proposals.title: Network Pharmacology of Lipid Mediators: A Multi-Omics and Functional Analysis of Endocannabinoid-Related Signaling Networks in Simvastatin-Induced Myotoxicity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Di Marzo's work, shows that statin-induced myotoxicity involves dysfunctional CB1 receptor signaling. However, focusing on single receptors overlooks the complex, interconnected nature of lipid mediator networks (e.g., endocannabinoids, N-acylethanolamines, fatty acid amides) that exhibit functional redundancy and crosstalk. A systems-level understanding is required to develop more effective and safer therapeutic strategies that modulate network homeostasis rather than single targets.
proposals.hypothesis: Simvastatin-induced skeletal muscle cell toxicity is driven by a coordinated dysregulation of a broader lipid mediator network, not solely by CB1 dysfunction, and pharmacological interventions targeting network hubs will confer superior cytoprotection compared to selective receptor agonists/antagonists.
proposals.method: We will use an in vitro model of simvastatin-induced toxicity in mouse C2C12 myotubes. Lipidomics (LC-MS/MS) will quantify temporal changes in a broad panel of lipid mediators (AEA, 2-AG, PEA, OEA, related N-acyl amides). Concurrent RNA-seq will profile expression changes of their metabolic enzymes and receptors. Data integration will be performed using weighted gene co-expression network analysis (WGCNA) and correlation network modeling to identify key network hubs. Validatory experiments will test cytoprotective effects of compounds (e.g., multi-target ligands or enzyme inhibitors) targeting identified hubs versus selective CB1 or TRPV1 agents.
proposals.contribution: This work will map the dysregulated lipid signaling network underlying statin myopathy, moving beyond a single-receptor view. It will identify novel network-based pharmacological targets and validate the therapeutic principle of 'lipid network stabilization'. The integrated multi-omics dataset will serve as a public resource for systems pharmacology in lipid research.
proposals.novelty: While Di Marzo's prior work identified CB1's role, this proposal innovates by applying a true network pharmacology framework to endocannabinoid-related biology, treating the lipidome as a dynamic, interconnected system rather than a collection of independent pathways, to explain and counteract drug toxicity.

proposals.title: Dissociating Neuroprotective from Immunomodulatory Effects of Cannabinoids in Experimental Autoimmune Encephalomyelitis: A Cell-Type Specific Receptor Knockout Approach
proposals.motivation: Cannabinoids show promise in treating multiple sclerosis (MS), but their therapeutic mechanisms remain conflated. Pre-2026 evidence indicates cannabinoids act on both immune cells and neurons via CB1 and CB2 receptors, making it unclear whether neurological benefits (e.g., neuroprotection, symptom relief) are direct or secondary to immune suppression. Clarifying this is critical for developing targeted therapies with fewer side effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the neurological benefits of cannabinoids in MS models are mediated directly via neuronal CB1 receptor signaling and are separable from the immunomodulatory effects mediated by CB1/CB2 on immune cells.
proposals.method: We will use the MOG35-55-induced Experimental Autoimmune Encephalomyelitis (EAE) model in mice. To isolate neuronal effects, we will employ conditional knockout mice lacking CB1 receptors specifically in neurons (using Synapsin-Cre) and in myeloid-lineage immune cells (using LysM-Cre). Mice will be treated with the selective CB1 agonist ACEA or vehicle. Disease progression will be scored clinically. Post-mortem, we will quantify neuroinflammation (IHC for microglia/astrocytes), neuronal damage (Fluoro-Jade C, synaptic markers), and demyelination (LFB-PAS staining). Immune cell profiling in the CNS and periphery will be performed via flow cytometry.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide definitive, causal evidence for or against a direct neuroprotective role of cannabinoid signaling in neuroinflammatory disease. It will delineate the primary cellular targets (neuronal vs. immune) responsible for specific therapeutic outcomes, informing the rational design of next-generation cannabinoid-based drugs for MS that aim to maximize efficacy while minimizing unwanted psychoactive or immunosuppressive effects.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has correlated cannabinoid administration with neuroprotection, this study uniquely employs cell-type-specific genetic ablation to causally dissect the contribution of neuronal versus immune CB1 signaling, moving beyond pharmacological correlation to mechanistic proof of separable pathways.

proposals.title: Targeted Psychoactivity as a Therapeutic Mechanism: Investigating CB1 Receptor-Dependent Signaling in a Mouse Model of Treatment-Resistant Depression
proposals.motivation: The clinical development of cannabinoid-based therapeutics has been heavily biased against psychoactivity, often viewed as an unavoidable side effect. This paradigm overlooks the potential that controlled, receptor-specific psychoactive signaling may directly contribute to therapeutic efficacy, particularly in neuropsychiatric disorders where conventional treatments fail. Re-evaluating this assumption is crucial for rational drug design and for unlocking the full therapeutic potential of the endocannabinoid system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, transient psychoactive effects mediated through central CB1 receptors are necessary for the rapid antidepressant and anxiolytic actions of certain cannabinoids, and that these effects can be dissociated from detrimental cognitive impairment through precise temporal and receptor-state control.
proposals.method: We will employ a chronic unpredictable mild stress (CUMS) mouse model of treatment-resistant depression. Using in vivo fiber photometry, we will record real-time neuronal activity in the prefrontal cortex and ventral hippocampus following administration of a selective CB1 agonist (e.g., AM-4054) and a non-psychoactive peripheral CB1 agonist (e.g., LH-21). Behavioral assays (sucrose preference, forced swim, elevated plus maze) will be conducted concurrently. We will use conditional knockout mice (CB1-floxed crossed with CaMKIIα-Cre) to isolate the role of forebrain glutamatergic neuron CB1 receptors. Signaling pathways (pERK, pCREB) will be analyzed ex vivo via western blot.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct experimental evidence linking specific psychoactive CB1 signaling to a measurable therapeutic outcome. It will establish a framework for defining 'therapeutic psychoactivity' versus 'adverse psychoactivity' based on neural circuit engagement. The findings could guide the development of next-generation cannabinoids that harness beneficial central effects while minimizing risks.
proposals.novelty: While the field seeks to avoid psychoactivity, this proposal deliberately investigates it as a potential therapeutic mechanism. It moves beyond correlation by using precise neuromodulation and circuit-specific genetic tools to test a causal role for psychoactive signaling in a disease model, a hypothesis not directly addressed in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Comprehensive Pharmacological Profiling of Δ9-Tetrahydrocannabivarin (THCV): Elucidating Agonist, Partial Agonist, and Off-Target Activity Beyond CB1 Antagonism
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 literature classifies THCV primarily as a CB1 receptor antagonist, but emerging evidence suggests its pharmacology is more complex and context-dependent, potentially involving off-target effects or functional selectivity. Given the therapeutic interest in THCV for metabolic and neurological disorders, a precise, systematic characterization of its signaling profile is critical to predict clinical efficacy, side effects, and potential drug-drug interactions, moving beyond the simplistic binary classification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that THCV exhibits context-dependent functional activity at cannabinoid receptors (CB1 and CB2) and possesses significant off-target interactions with other members of the endocannabinoidome (e.g., TRP channels, GPR55), contributing to its in vivo effects beyond simple CB1 antagonism.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-tiered approach: 1) In vitro signaling assays in HEK293 cells stably expressing human CB1 or CB2 receptors to measure G protein activation (using BRET-based biosensors), β-arrestin recruitment, and ERK phosphorylation, establishing full concentration-response curves to determine efficacy and potency. 2) Assessment of activity at TRPV1, TRPV2, and GPR55 using calcium flux and β-arrestin recruitment assays. 3) Ex vivo electrophysiology on mouse brain slices to evaluate THCV's functional impact on synaptic transmission in CB1-expressing neurons, comparing effects to pure antagonists like rimonabant. 4) In silico molecular docking studies using published CB1 and TRPV1 structures to predict binding poses that may explain functional selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated, quantitative pharmacological signature of THCV across key signaling pathways, resolving controversies in the literature. It will directly inform the rational development of THCV-based therapeutics by identifying its precise molecular targets and signaling biases, enabling better prediction of its therapeutic window and potential side-effect profile.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have examined isolated aspects of THCV activity, this proposal systematically integrates modern signaling bias assays with neuronal function and computational docking, a comprehensive strategy not yet applied to this phytocannabinoid, to definitively map its polypharmacology within the endocannabinoidome.

proposals.title: A Phase IIa, Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial to Assess the Efficacy and Safety of Cannabidiol on Neuroinflammation and Endocannabinoid Tone in Patients with Mild Cognitive Impairment due to Alzheimer’s Disease
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence indicates that neuroinflammation and endocannabinoid system (ECS) dysregulation are key pathological drivers in Alzheimer’s disease (AD). Cannabidiol (CBD) has shown promising anti-inflammatory, neuroprotective, and ECS-modulating properties in preclinical models, but robust clinical data in early AD stages is lacking. This trial addresses a critical translational gap by moving from animal and in silico studies to a targeted human clinical investigation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chronic administration of CBD to patients with mild cognitive impairment (MCI) due to AD will reduce central and peripheral markers of neuroinflammation and restore homeostatic endocannabinoid signaling, correlating with improved cognitive performance and neuropsychiatric symptom scores.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 24-week trial with 60 MCI-AD patients randomized to oral CBD (600 mg/day) or placebo. Primary outcomes will be changes in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) and endocannabinoids (anandamide, 2-AG). Secondary outcomes include cognitive assessments (ADAS-Cog), neuropsychiatric inventory scores, and plasma endocannabinoid/cytokine profiling. Advanced statistical modeling (mixed-effects models) will correlate ECS tone modulation with clinical and biomarker changes, building on our group's expertise in ECS signaling and receptor pharmacology.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct clinical evidence linking CBD’s therapeutic effects to modulation of the ECS and neuroinflammation in AD. It will establish crucial pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic relationships for CBD in this population and identify predictive biomarkers for future larger-scale trials.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior trials focusing solely on behavioral outcomes, this proposal uniquely integrates deep ECS biomarker analysis (CSF/plasma) with clinical metrics, directly testing the mechanistic link between CBD, ECS homeostasis, and neuroinflammation in a well-defined early AD cohort.

proposals.title: A Microfluidic Gut-Brain Axis-on-a-Chip Platform for High-Throughput Screening of Cannabinoid Efficacy and Toxicity in Human Tissues
proposals.motivation: Preclinical screening of cannabinoids and endocannabinoid system modulators relies heavily on animal models and isolated cell cultures, which poorly recapitulate human tissue complexity and inter-organ communication, such as the gut-brain axis. This gap hinders the efficient development of targeted therapies for gastrointestinal and neurological disorders. Organ-on-a-chip technology, established pre-2026, offers a promising alternative by enabling physiologically relevant, human-based tissue models with controlled microenvironments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a microfluidic gut-liver-brain axis-on-a-chip platform will reveal tissue-specific and inter-organ signaling-dependent efficacy and toxicity profiles of synthetic and natural cannabinoids that are not predictable from conventional mono-culture systems.
proposals.method: We will engineer a tri-compartmental polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) microfluidic device to house human intestinal epithelial cells (Caco-2), primary human hepatocyte spheroids, and human induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived neurons with functional CB1/TRPV1 receptor expression, connected via microchannels permitting shared medium flow. Cannabinoids (e.g., specific CB1 agonists/antagonists, TRPV1 agonists from prior in-silico designs) will be perfused, and real-time readouts will include calcium imaging for neuronal signaling, transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) for gut barrier integrity, and LC-MS analysis of secreted endocannabinoids (e.g., 2-AG, AEA) and inflammatory cytokines.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, human-relevant screening platform that simultaneously assesses cannabinoid effects on multiple target tissues, providing a systems-level view of efficacy and potential hepatotoxicity or neurotoxicity. It will generate crucial data on how gut-derived signals modulate central cannabinoid responses, directly informing the rational design of safer and more effective endocannabinoid-based therapeutics.
proposals.novelty: While organ-on-a-chip models exist, their application for screening cannabinoid effects has been limited to single tissues. This proposal is novel in integrating gut, liver, and neural tissues to specifically model the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of cannabinoids across a human-relevant biological axis, moving beyond static receptor expression studies.

proposals.title: Engineering a Yeast (S. cerevisiae) Platform for the High-Yield Biosynthesis of Tetrahydrocannabivarin (THCV) via Targeted Pathway Optimization and Receptor Activity Profiling
proposals.motivation: Minor cannabinoids like THCV show unique and promising pharmacological profiles, including CB1 antagonism and potential anti-obesity effects, but their study and therapeutic application are hindered by extremely low natural abundance in Cannabis sativa. Pre-2026 metabolic engineering efforts have established yeast platforms for major cannabinoids (THC, CBD), yet scalable production of specific, rare analogs like THCV remains inefficient and costly, limiting preclinical and clinical research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by engineering the precursor supply for propionyl-CoA and optimizing the expression and coupling of specific cannabinoid acid synthases (CAS) with a tailored olivetolic acid (OA) pathway in yeast, we can achieve high-titer, sustainable production of tetrahydrocannabivarin carboxylic acid (THCVA), which can be functionally validated using cannabinoid receptor signaling assays.
proposals.method: We will engineer Saccharomyces cerevisiae to overproduce the THCV precursor, divarinolic acid, by integrating a heterologous pathway for propionyl-CoA synthesis and expressing a mutated polyketide synthase (PKS) and olivetolic acid cyclase (OAC) with altered substrate specificity. This will be coupled with a Cannabis-derived cannabigerovarinic acid (CBGVA) synthase and a specific THCV synthase. We will use CRISPR-Cas9 for genomic integration and employ adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) to enhance precursor flux. The purified THCVA will be decarboxylated to THCV, and its activity will be confirmed using CB1 and CB2 receptor β-arrestin recruitment assays in HEK293 cells, a method aligned with the researcher's expertise.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable, fermentation-based production system for a pharmacologically important rare cannabinoid, providing a sustainable and consistent material source for biomedical research. It will also generate new insights into the substrate specificity and engineering constraints of the cannabinoid biosynthetic enzyme family, advancing synthetic biology for natural product discovery.
proposals.novelty: While yeast platforms for cannabinoids exist, this proposal specifically targets the high-yield production of the propyl-side-chain analog THCV through directed pathway engineering for propionyl-CoA, a non-native precursor in yeast, combined with functional validation using receptor signaling assays, bridging metabolic engineering with pharmacological characterization.

proposals.title: Rational Design, Synthesis, and Pharmacological Profiling of Novel CBD Analogues Targeting Underexplored Molecular Scaffolds for Enhanced Cannabinoid Receptor Modulatory Activity
proposals.motivation: Cannabidiol (CBD) exhibits therapeutic potential for pain, inflammation, and neurological disorders, but its low-affinity, multi-target pharmacology limits potency and specificity. Pre-2026 medicinal chemistry efforts have predominantly focused on modifications at the C-7 and C-5' positions of the CBD scaffold, leaving other regions of the molecule largely unexplored for optimizing cannabinoid receptor (CB1/CB2) interaction and signaling bias. Systematic exploration of these underexamined chemical spaces is needed to develop more potent and selective CBD-derived pharmacological tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strategic chemical modifications to the CBD core structure beyond the traditional C-7 and C-5' positions—particularly on the terpene ring and the alkyl side chain—will yield novel analogues with significantly enhanced binding affinity, functional selectivity (biased agonism/antagonism), and improved metabolic stability compared to the parent compound.
proposals.method: We will employ an in silico-guided rational design approach, using molecular docking (e.g., AutoDock Vina) and molecular dynamics simulations on pre-2026 CB1 and CB2 receptor models to predict binding poses and affinity of designed analogues. This will guide the synthesis of a focused library of 20-30 novel CBD derivatives with systematic modifications to the pentyl side chain (e.g., cyclization, heteroatom incorporation, length variation) and the terpene ring (e.g., decarboxylation, heterocyclic ring fusion). Compounds will be pharmacologically profiled using radioligand binding assays (CB1/CB2), functional assays (cAMP accumulation, β-arrestin recruitment) in transfected HEK-293 cells, and tested for cytotoxicity in murine C2C12 myotubes to assess therapeutic index.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new library of chemically novel CBD analogues with defined cannabinoid receptor pharmacology, providing critical structure-activity relationship (SAR) data for underexplored regions of the CBD scaffold. It will identify lead compounds with improved potency or signaling bias, offering superior molecular tools to dissect endocannabinoid system function and potential starting points for future drug development.
proposals.novelty: The proposal moves beyond the well-trodden C-7/C-5' modification strategy to pioneer a systematic exploration of the alkyl side chain and terpene ring core of CBD, a significantly underexplored avenue in pre-2026 cannabinoid medicinal chemistry, integrated with a comprehensive signaling bias profile.

proposals.title: Synergistic Analgesic and Anti-inflammatory Effects of Combined TRPV1 Agonism and Anandamide Reuptake Inhibition in Murine Models of Neuropathic Pain
proposals.motivation: The endocannabinoid (eCB) and transient receptor potential vanilloid 1 (TRPV1, historically VR1) systems are key, interacting modulators of pain and inflammation. Pre-2026 research shows that anandamide (AEA) activates both CB1 receptors and TRPV1, but its therapeutic potential is limited by rapid cellular uptake. Simultaneously targeting TRPV1 and inhibiting AEA degradation/uptake could produce enhanced, multi-modal therapeutic effects, a strategy not fully explored for neuropathic pain.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that co-administration of a selective TRPV1 agonist and an AEA reuptake inhibitor will produce synergistic analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects in mouse models of neuropathic pain, surpassing the efficacy of either intervention alone, by concurrently enhancing TRPV1-mediated desensitization and prolonging CB1 receptor-mediated signaling.
proposals.method: We will use a mouse model of chronic constriction injury (CCI)-induced neuropathic pain. Mice will receive systemic or intraplantar administration of: a) vehicle, b) a novel, potent TRPV1 agonist (e.g., a 4-(thiophen-2-yl)butanamide derivative), c) the AEA reuptake inhibitor URB597, or d) their combination. Pain behaviors (mechanical allodynia, thermal hyperalgesia) will be assessed. Spinal cord and dorsal root ganglia will be analyzed for markers of neuroinflammation (e.g., GFAP, Iba1, cytokines) using immunohistochemistry and ELISA. In vitro, calcium imaging and whole-cell patch-clamp recordings on cultured DRG neurons will characterize functional interactions between TRPV1 activation and eCB signaling pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive in vivo evidence for a synergistic therapeutic interaction between TRPV1 agonism and AEA reuptake inhibition. It will define the cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying this synergy, offering a novel, potentially more effective and lower-dose combinatorial strategy for managing neuropathic pain and inflammation.
proposals.novelty: While interactions between the eCB and TRPV1 systems are known, the deliberate pharmacological combination of a selective TRPV1 agonist (not a dual agonist like AEA) with an AEA reuptake inhibitor to create a synergistic, targeted 'entourage effect' is a novel therapeutic concept not previously tested in neuropathic pain models.

proposals.title: Predictive QSAR Modeling and In Vitro Validation of Novel CBD Analogues for Enhanced and Selective CB2 Receptor Activity
proposals.motivation: Cannabidiol (CBD) shows therapeutic potential in neuroinflammation and pain, but its polypharmacology and low potency at cannabinoid receptors limit clinical application. Rational design of optimized analogues is hindered by a lack of comprehensive structure-activity relationship (SAR) models that can predict activity at specific endocannabinoid system (ECS) targets, particularly the CB2 receptor, which is a promising target for immunomodulation without psychoactive effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying advanced QSAR modeling to a curated library of CBD analogues, we can identify novel chemical scaffolds with predicted high affinity and efficacy for the CB2 receptor, and that these predictions will be validated in vitro, yielding new selective CB2 ligands.
proposals.method: We will curate a dataset of known CBD analogues with associated CB1 and CB2 receptor affinity/efficacy data from literature and in-house assays. Molecular descriptors (e.g., 3D pharmacophore, electronic, topological) will be calculated using Dragon or MOE software. Machine learning models, including Random Forest and Support Vector Regression (SVR), will be trained to predict CB2 activity. The best model will guide the in silico design of new analogues, which will be synthesized and tested for binding (radioligand displacement) and functional activity (cAMP and β-arrestin recruitment assays) in CB1- and CB2-expressing cell lines.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated predictive QSAR model for CB2 activity, accelerating the discovery of novel ECS modulators. It will also provide a set of newly synthesized, potent, and selective CB2 agonist candidates, offering new chemical tools and potential lead compounds for treating CB2-mediated pathologies.
proposals.novelty: While QSAR has been applied to classical cannabinoids, a dedicated model focusing on the optimization of CBD's scaffold specifically for CB2 selectivity is lacking. This approach integrates modern machine-learning QSAR with the team's expertise in ECS pharmacology and medicinal chemistry, as evidenced by recent work on TRPV1 and CB1 receptor ligands.

proposals.title: Rational Design and Synthesis of Novel CBD Analogues as Potent Nanomolar Inhibitors of Anandamide Cellular Uptake
proposals.motivation: Anandamide (AEA) is a key endocannabinoid whose extracellular levels are primarily regulated by a selective, facilitated cellular uptake process. Current inhibitors of this uptake, including CBD and its analogues, typically exhibit only micromolar potency, limiting their utility as pharmacological tools and potential therapeutics. Developing potent, nanomolar inhibitors is crucial for precisely dissecting AEA signaling in physiology and disease, and for validating the uptake process as a therapeutic target for pain and inflammation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic structural modification of the CBD scaffold, guided by computational modeling of its interaction with the putative AEA transporter, will yield novel analogues with significantly enhanced (nanomolar) affinity and selectivity for inhibiting AEA cellular uptake.
proposals.method: We will employ an in silico-guided rational drug design strategy. Using known CBD-based uptake inhibitors as a starting point, we will perform molecular docking and molecular dynamics simulations against homology models of proposed uptake protein targets (e.g., EMT/FABP5). This will inform the synthesis of novel 4-(thiophen-2-yl)butanamide and other heterocyclic analogues. New compounds will be screened in vitro for AEA uptake inhibition in neuronal (e.g., N18TG2) and immune cell lines using radiolabeled AEA, with selectivity assessed against CB1, CB2, and TRPV1 receptors.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first rationally designed, nanomolar-potency inhibitors of AEA uptake, providing a transformative pharmacological tool to study endocannabinoid tone. It will offer critical structural insights into the ligand-binding site of the uptake mechanism and establish a robust chemical platform for developing potential therapeutics targeting endocannabinoid reuptake.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted application of advanced computational chemistry specifically to the CBD scaffold for AEA uptake inhibition, moving beyond serendipitous discovery, and the explicit aim of achieving nanomolar potency—an order-of-magnitude improvement over all pre-2026 inhibitors.

proposals.title: Rational Design and Pharmacological Characterization of Novel Dual-Action Compounds Targeting TRPV1 Antagonism and Anandamide Reuptake Inhibition
proposals.motivation: The endocannabinoid system (ECS) and transient receptor potential vanilloid 1 (TRPV1) channel are key, interconnected players in pain and inflammation. While TRPV1 antagonists show analgesic potential, their clinical development has been hampered by hyperthermia side effects. Enhancing endogenous anandamide (AEA) tone via uptake inhibition provides analgesia with a potentially superior safety profile. A dual-target compound that blocks TRPV1 while inhibiting AEA uptake could offer a synergistic, multi-modal therapeutic strategy for chronic pain with minimized adverse effects, addressing a significant unmet clinical need.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rationally designed small molecules can be identified which simultaneously act as TRPV1 antagonists and potent inhibitors of the anandamide membrane transporter (EMT), creating a unique pharmacological profile that synergistically modulates pain pathways.
proposals.method: We will employ an in silico-guided rational drug design strategy, building on our expertise with 4-(thiophen-2-yl)butanamides as TRPV1 ligands. Using structure-activity relationship (SAR) data and molecular docking against homology models of TRPV1 and the putative EMT, we will design and synthesize a focused library of novel compounds. These will be screened in vitro for TRPV1 antagonism (using calcium imaging in TRPV1-expressing HEK293 cells and rat dorsal root ganglion neurons) and for AEA uptake inhibition (using radiolabeled [³H]AEA uptake assays in RBL-2H3 and neuronal cells). Lead compounds will be evaluated in murine models of inflammatory and neuropathic pain.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first pharmacological proof-of-concept for a dual TRPV1 antagonist/AEA uptake inhibitor, providing novel chemical tools to dissect ECS-TRPV1 crosstalk. It will establish a structure-activity framework for multi-target endocannabinoid system modulators and identify a promising lead candidate for the treatment of chronic pain conditions.
proposals.novelty: While selective TRPV1 antagonists and AEA uptake inhibitors exist, the deliberate design of a single chemical entity combining these two specific, mechanistically distinct actions is a novel and non-obvious strategy to achieve enhanced efficacy and a potentially improved side-effect profile through synergistic endocannabinoid modulation.

proposals.title: Targeting TRPV1 and CB1 Receptor Subpopulations in Dorsal Root Ganglia for the Differential Management of Pleural Pain
proposals.motivation: Pleural pain, a debilitating symptom of pleurisy and thoracic malignancies, is poorly managed by existing analgesics, which often lack efficacy or cause significant side effects. Pre-2026 research has identified distinct nociceptor subpopulations in dorsal root ganglia (DRG) innervating the pleura, but the specific endocannabinoid (eCB) and transient receptor potential vanilloid 1 (TRPV1) signaling profiles of these neurons remain undefined, hindering targeted therapy development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pleural pain is mediated by specific subpopulations of pleural-projecting DRG neurons defined by their co-expression patterns of TRPV1 and cannabinoid CB1 receptors, and that selectively modulating these receptors with tailored agonists/antagonists will produce differentiated, subpopulation-specific analgesia.
proposals.method: We will use retrograde tracing from the parietal pleura in mice to identify pleural-innervating DRG neurons. Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) of these isolated neurons will define transcriptional subpopulations, focusing on *Trpv1*, *Cnr1* (CB1), and related signaling genes. Based on this map, we will employ in silico-guided rational drug design, leveraging known pharmacophores from Dr. Di Marzo's prior TRPV1 agonist work, to synthesize novel compounds with predicted selectivity for identified co-expression profiles. These compounds will be tested in vitro on TRPV1/CB1-transfected cells and in vivo in a mouse model of carrageenan-induced pleurisy, assessing pain behaviors (grimace, guarding) and neuronal activation (c-Fos).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first molecular and functional map of eCB/TRPV1 systems in pleural nociceptors. It will deliver novel, rationally designed lead compounds that selectively target defined neuronal subpopulations, moving beyond non-selective blockade. The project will establish a preclinical proof-of-concept for a stratified approach to visceral pain therapy.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating scRNA-seq-based subpopulation mapping of pleural nociceptors with a rational drug design pipeline specifically aimed at their unique TRPV1/CB1 co-expression signatures, a strategy not yet applied to pleural pain.

proposals.title: Causality of Anandamide Surge in Stress Response: Determining if AEA Elevation is a Protective Trigger or a Secondary Biomarker in Neuronal Models
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research establishes that anandamide (AEA) levels rise rapidly in response to various cellular stressors, often correlating with adaptive or protective outcomes. However, it remains unresolved whether this AEA surge is the primary causal trigger initiating the protective cascade or merely a secondary consequence of the stress response. Determining this causality is critical for understanding the fundamental role of the endocannabinoid system in cellular homeostasis and for developing targeted therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: The rapid rise in anandamide following stress induction is a causative protective signal, not a passive biomarker. We hypothesize that preventing the acute AEA rise will abolish subsequent protective adaptations, while artificially inducing an AEA rise prior to stress will mimic protection.
proposals.method: We will use primary cortical neuron cultures from wild-type and conditional CB1 receptor knockout mice. Stress will be induced via simvastatin (as per Di Marzo's recent work) or glutamate excitotoxicity. AEA levels will be manipulated pharmacologically using FAAH inhibitors (e.g., URB597) to elevate AEA, and selective AEA synthesis inhibitors (targeting NAPE-PLD) to block its rise. Real-time AEA dynamics will be monitored via LC-MS/MS. Neuronal viability, calcium signaling, and downstream protective pathways (e.g., pCREB, BDNF) will be assessed. The temporal sequence of events will be analyzed to establish causality.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively establish the causal role, if any, of acute anandamide signaling in neuronal stress adaptation. It will clarify the primary function of this rapid endocannabinoid response, directly informing whether therapeutic strategies should aim to boost AEA signaling proactively or modulate its consequences. The findings will refine models of endocannabinoid system operation in neuroprotection.
proposals.novelty: While correlative links between AEA and protection are well-known, this study directly tests temporal and causal precedence using precise pharmacological tools to isolate the AEA surge, moving beyond association to mechanism.

proposals.title: Identification of CB1-Independent Neuroprotective Signaling Pathways in a Murine Model of Excitotoxic Stress
proposals.motivation: The endocannabinoid system, particularly via CB1 receptor activation, provides crucial on-demand neuroprotection. However, in conditions of chronic CB1 blockade or dysfunction, as seen in certain metabolic or iatrogenic disorders, neurons may still survive, suggesting the existence of compensatory pathways. Identifying these pathways is critical for developing novel therapeutic strategies for neurodegenerative diseases and managing side effects of drugs like statins that impair CB1 signaling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in the absence of functional CB1 receptor signaling, neurons activate alternative receptor systems, such as TRPV1, PPARs, or other GPCRs coupled to similar intracellular cascades, to maintain neuroprotective homeostasis against excitotoxic insults.
proposals.method: We will employ primary cortical neuron cultures from wild-type and conditional CB1-knockout mice, subjecting them to NMDA-induced excitotoxicity. Using a combination of targeted lipidomics (LC-MS/MS) to profile endocannabinoid-like lipid mediators and unbiased RNA-seq, we will identify differentially expressed genes and altered lipid signaling pathways in CB1-deficient neurons under stress. Key candidate pathways (e.g., TRPV1, PPARγ) will be validated pharmacologically using selective agonists/antagonists and by monitoring neuronal survival, calcium imaging, and phosphorylation states of shared downstream effectors (e.g., pAkt, pERK).
proposals.contribution: This work will map the specific signaling networks that compensate for lost CB1 function, revealing new targets for neuroprotection. It will expand the understanding of endocannabinoid system plasticity and provide a mechanistic basis for treating conditions where CB1 signaling is compromised.
proposals.novelty: While CB1's role is well-studied, systematic investigation of its functional redundancy in a defined excitotoxicity model, integrating lipidomic and transcriptomic analyses to uncover compensatory systems, is novel and moves beyond the canonical endocannabinoid framework.

proposals.title: Optogenetic Induction of Excitotoxicity in Cortical Glutamatergic Neurons Reveals a Spatiotemporal Role for CB1 Receptor-Mediated Neuroprotection
proposals.motivation: Excitotoxicity, driven by excessive glutamate release, is a core pathological mechanism in stroke and neurodegenerative diseases. The endocannabinoid system, via presynaptic CB1 receptor activation, is known to inhibit glutamate release, but its precise spatiotemporal dynamics and sufficiency for neuroprotection during defined excitotoxic insults remain unclear. A major pre-2026 limitation is the inability to trigger excitotoxicity with cellular and temporal precision to isolate CB1's role.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that optogenetically triggered, temporally precise excitotoxicity in defined glutamatergic neurons will induce neuronal damage that can be mitigated by enhancing endocannabinoid-CB1 receptor signaling, and that this protection is dependent on the precise timing of CB1 activation relative to the excitotoxic stimulus.
proposals.method: We will express channelrhodopsin-2 (ChR2) specifically in cortical glutamatergic neurons of mice using viral vectors in Camk2a-Cre lines. In acute brain slices and in vivo, controlled blue-light pulses will induce sustained depolarization and glutamate release, mimicking excitotoxicity. Neuronal damage will be quantified via live imaging of propidium iodide uptake and post-hoc immunohistochemistry for cleaved caspase-3. The role of CB1 will be tested using CB1 knockout mice, the selective agonist ACEA, and the FAAH inhibitor URB597 to elevate endogenous anandamide levels, applied at specific time windows relative to light stimulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, precisely controllable model of excitotoxicity, moving beyond broad chemical agonists. It will define the critical time window for CB1-mediated neuroprotection and demonstrate whether boosting endocannabinoid tone can be a viable strategy against defined excitotoxic events. The findings will directly inform targeted therapeutic approaches for conditions like ischemic stroke.
proposals.novelty: While CB1's role in neuroprotection is known, this study is novel in using optogenetics to induce excitotoxicity with millisecond precision in a defined neuronal population, allowing an unprecedented examination of the causal relationship and temporal requirements of CB1 signaling in a controlled pathological context.

proposals.title: Whole-Brain Mapping of CB1 Receptor-Dependent Neural Circuits Mediating Seizure Protection
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that the endocannabinoid system, via CB1 receptor activation, exerts potent anticonvulsant effects, primarily studied in hippocampal circuits. However, the broader network of CB1-dependent protective circuits across the whole brain during seizures remains largely unmapped. Understanding this global network is crucial for developing targeted neuromodulatory therapies for epilepsy that move beyond focal hippocampal interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CB1 receptor activation during seizures recruits a distributed, coordinated network of brain regions beyond the hippocampus, and that the functional connectivity within this network is essential for mediating seizure suppression and behavioral protection.
proposals.method: We will use wide-field calcium imaging in Thy1-GCaMP6f transgenic mice in vivo to capture whole-brain cortical and subcortical (via optical clearing) activity dynamics during pentylenetetrazole (PTZ)-induced seizures. CB1-specific modulation will be achieved using the selective agonist ACEA and antagonist rimonabant, administered systemically or via localized microinjections. Activity maps will be analyzed using custom algorithms for region-of-interest (ROI) segmentation based on the Allen Brain Atlas, followed by Granger causality and correlation matrix analysis to identify CB1-dependent functional connectivity changes during ictal events.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, real-time map of CB1-dependent whole-brain activity during seizures, identifying novel nodes in the anticonvulsant network. It will define how endocannabinoid signaling coordinates distributed neural ensembles to control hyperexcitability, offering a systems-level framework for future therapeutic strategies targeting circuit-level dysfunction in epilepsy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on CB1 effects in isolated regions like the hippocampus, this proposal applies cutting-edge, pre-2026 whole-brain imaging technology to reveal the system-wide, circuit-based mechanism of endocannabinoid-mediated seizure protection, a perspective not yet explored.

proposals.title: Ontogeny of the On-Demand Endocannabinoid System: Functional Characterization of CB1 Receptor-Mediated Neuroprotection in the Neonatal Mouse Brain
proposals.motivation: The endocannabinoid system (ECS) is a crucial on-demand neuroprotective mechanism in the adult brain, mitigating excitotoxicity and oxidative stress. However, its functional maturation and protective capacity during early postnatal development, a period of heightened vulnerability to insults like hypoxia-ischemia, remain poorly characterized. Understanding whether this system is fully operational in the neonatal brain is critical for identifying age-specific therapeutic targets for neurodevelopmental disorders and perinatal brain injury.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the on-demand, activity-dependent ECS signaling through CB1 receptors is functionally distinct and less efficacious in the neonatal brain compared to the adult, due to developmental differences in receptor expression, localization, and coupling to downstream protective pathways.
proposals.method: We will use a combination of in vivo and ex vivo approaches in C57BL/6 mice at postnatal days P7, P14, and P60 (adult). First, we will quantify developmental changes in CB1 receptor protein and mRNA levels in cortical and hippocampal synaptosomes via Western blot and qPCR. Second, using acute brain slices, we will employ electrophysiology (patch-clamp) to measure depolarization-induced suppression of excitation (DSE) and inhibition (DSI) as functional readouts of on-demand endocannabinoid signaling. Finally, we will induce excitotoxic stress in slices with NMDA and assess neuroprotection via CB1 agonism (WIN55,212-2) and antagonism (AM251), measuring neuronal death with propidium iodide staining.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive functional map of on-demand ECS neuroprotection across key postnatal developmental stages. It will define the developmental timeline of CB1-mediated protective efficacy and identify potential critical periods of ECS vulnerability. The findings will establish a foundational knowledge base for considering ECS-targeted interventions in the developing brain.
proposals.novelty: While the neuroprotective role of the ECS in adults is established, its functional capacity during the dynamic phase of synaptogenesis and circuit refinement in the neonate is unknown. This proposal directly tests the on-demand, activity-driven aspect of the system—its core physiological function—in a developmental context, moving beyond mere expression profiling.

proposals.title: Spatial Mapping of Endocannabinoid Enzyme Dysregulation in Human Type 2 Diabetic Pancreas Using MALDI Mass Spectrometry Imaging
proposals.motivation: The endocannabinoid system (ECS) is hyperactive in type 2 diabetes (T2D), contributing to metabolic dysfunction, but its precise role in pancreatic pathology remains unclear. Bulk tissue analyses obscure critical spatial information, failing to reveal if ECS dysregulation is localized to specific micro-anatomical regions like β-cell cores, islet mantles, or peri-islet acinar tissue. A spatially resolved molecular map is essential to understand the regional biochemical pathology driving islet dysfunction and peripheral insulin resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dysregulation of key endocannabinoid synthetic (NAPE-PLD, DAGLα) and degradative (FAAH, MAGL) enzymes in T2D is not uniform across the pancreas but is spatially compartmentalized within specific islet micro-domains and peri-islet regions, correlating with local inflammatory or metabolic stress.
proposals.method: We will perform Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization Mass Spectrometry Imaging (MALDI-MSI) on frozen pancreatic tissue sections from human T2D and non-diabetic donors. Tissue will be coated with a matrix suitable for lipid and peptide detection. We will target enzyme expression by imaging specific tryptic peptides unique to NAPE-PLD, DAGLα, FAAH, and MAGL, using on-tissue enzymatic digestion protocols validated in neurobiology. Spatial distributions will be coregistered with consecutive sections immunostained for insulin, glucagon, and CD68 to define islet architecture and macrophage infiltration. Quantitative spatial analysis will be performed using SCiLS Lab software.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first spatially resolved atlas of ECS enzyme expression in the human diabetic pancreas, directly linking enzyme dysregulation to specific cellular niches. It will identify novel, region-specific therapeutic targets within the ECS for preserving β-cell function or modulating peri-islet inflammation. The work will establish a powerful MSI framework for spatial metabolomics-lipidomics in endocrine pancreas research.
proposals.novelty: While MSI has been used to map neurotransmitters in brain, its application to map endocannabinoid system enzymes in the pancreas is novel. This moves beyond bulk measurements or single-cell RNA-seq by preserving the native tissue architecture to reveal paracrine signaling micro-environments critical for understanding ECS pathology in T2D.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Transcriptomic Profiling of the Endocannabinoid System in Murine and Human Pancreatic Islets
proposals.motivation: The endocannabinoid system (ECS) is a potent modulator of energy metabolism, and its overactivity is implicated in obesity and type 2 diabetes. While pharmacological and genetic studies suggest ECS components influence insulin and glucagon secretion, a comprehensive, cell-type-specific map of ECS gene expression (e.g., Cnr1, Cnr2, DAGLa/b, Nape-pld, Faah) within the heterogeneous pancreatic islet is lacking. Defining this molecular anatomy is a critical first step to understanding how local endocannabinoid signaling fine-tunes islet hormone release and contributes to metabolic disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ECS component genes are expressed in a cell-type-specific manner within pancreatic islets, with distinct profiles in alpha, beta, delta, and gamma (PP) cells, and that these expression patterns are dysregulated in models of metabolic stress.
proposals.method: We will perform single-cell RNA-sequencing (scRNA-seq) on isolated pancreatic islets from wild-type and diet-induced obese (DIO) mice, and on human islets from non-diabetic and type 2 diabetic donors, using the 10x Genomics Chromium platform. Cell types will be identified by canonical markers (Ins1, Gcg, Sst, Ppy). We will quantify the expression of a curated panel of ECS genes (receptors, synthesizing and degrading enzymes) using established bioinformatics pipelines (Seurat v4 in R) for clustering, differential expression analysis, and trajectory inference to assess ECS dynamics in stressed beta-cells.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution atlas of ECS expression across all major islet cell types in both mice and humans. It will identify which cell types autonomously produce or degrade endocannabinoids and which are primary targets of cannabinoid signaling. Furthermore, by comparing healthy and diabetic states, we will generate testable hypotheses about how dysregulated islet ECS gene networks contribute to endocrine dysfunction.
proposals.novelty: Previous work on the islet ECS relied on bulk tissue analysis or pharmacological probes, obscuring cell-specific roles. Our application of scRNA-seq directly addresses this gap, moving beyond the assumption that ECS effects are solely neuronal or systemic to define the intrinsic islet 'cannabinoidome' with unprecedented precision.

proposals.title: Microfluidic Co-culture Reveals Adipocyte-Derived Endocannabinoid Signaling as a Paracrine Modulator of Beta-Cell Function and Viability
proposals.motivation: The endocannabinoid system (ECS) is hyperactive in obesity, contributing to metabolic dysregulation. Pre-2026 evidence links elevated adipose tissue endocannabinoid (eCB) levels to insulin resistance, but the direct paracrine impact of adipocyte-derived eCBs on pancreatic beta-cell function and survival remains poorly understood. This adipose-islet cross-talk is a critical, understudied axis in the pathogenesis of type 2 diabetes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adipocytes, under conditions mimicking nutrient overload, release eCBs (e.g., 2-AG, AEA) that act in a paracrine manner on cannabinoid receptors (CB1R, TRPV1) on beta-cells, thereby impairing glucose-stimulated insulin secretion and promoting apoptotic signaling.
proposals.method: We will establish a microfluidic co-culture system (e.g., using a commercially available OrganoPlate) to compartmentalize 3T3-L1-derived adipocytes and MIN6 beta-cells or primary mouse islets, allowing shared medium flow. Adipocytes will be pre-conditioned with high glucose/palmitate. We will quantify eCB levels (LC-MS/MS) in the conditioned medium and assess beta-cell function (dynamic GSIS assays) and viability (caspase-3/7 activity). Pharmacological and genetic (siRNA) interventions targeting CB1R, CB2R, and TRPV1 on beta-cells will pinpoint receptor-specific contributions. Signaling pathways (e.g., pAkt, pERK) will be analyzed by western blot.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence of a functional, paracrine eCB-mediated dialogue between adipose and endocrine pancreas. It will identify the specific eCB species and receptor subtypes involved, defining a novel mechanistic link between adipose tissue dysfunction and beta-cell failure. The findings could validate new therapeutic targets (e.g., peripheral CB1R antagonists) for preserving beta-cell mass in diabetes.
proposals.novelty: While eCBs are known systemic mediators, this study uniquely employs a controlled microfluidic co-culture to isolate and characterize their role as local adipose-derived paracrine factors directly targeting beta-cells, moving beyond correlative plasma level studies.

proposals.title: Endocannabinoid Signaling Orchestrates Mesenchymal Stem Cell Fate Decisions in Peripheral Adipose Tissue: A Role for CB1 and TRPV1 Receptors
proposals.motivation: The endocannabinoid system (ECS) is a key regulator of energy metabolism and tissue homeostasis in peripheral organs, with dysregulation linked to metabolic disorders. However, its direct role in controlling the proliferation and differentiation of resident stem and progenitor cells in these tissues remains poorly defined. Elucidating this role is crucial for understanding tissue repair mechanisms and developing novel regenerative therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that endocannabinoids, via a dynamic balance between CB1 receptor-mediated inhibition and TRPV1 receptor-mediated activation, critically regulate the fate decisions of adipose-derived mesenchymal stem cells (AD-MSCs), steering them towards adipogenic or osteogenic differentiation.
proposals.method: We will isolate and culture primary AD-MSCs from wild-type, CB1-/-, and TRPV1-/- mice. Using pharmacological (selective agonists/antagonists) and genetic manipulation (siRNA), we will modulate CB1 and TRPV1 signaling. Cell fate will be assessed by quantifying proliferation (BrdU/EdU incorporation) and differentiation (Oil Red O and Alizarin Red staining, plus qPCR for lineage-specific markers). Signaling pathways (e.g., MAPK/ERK, AKT) will be analyzed via western blot. Data will be integrated using a computational model to predict ECS-mediated fate outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, mechanistic link between ECS tone and stem cell fate in a key peripheral organ, defining a novel homeostatic function for the ECS beyond neuronal signaling. It will provide a foundational framework for targeting the ECS in stem cell-based therapies for metabolic bone and adipose tissue disorders.
proposals.novelty: While ECS effects on mature cell function are known, its specific role in adult stem cell fate decisions in peripheral organs is unexplored. The proposed antagonistic interplay between CB1 and TRPV1 receptors in this context is a novel, non-obvious regulatory paradigm.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Metabolic Feedback Loop: Assessing the Central Nervous System Impact of Peripherally Restricted CB1 Receptor Modulation
proposals.motivation: The therapeutic potential of peripherally restricted CB1 antagonists/inverse agonists for metabolic disorders is significant, aiming to avoid the neuropsychiatric side effects of brain-penetrant drugs like rimonabant. However, pre-2026 evidence suggests that peripheral CB1 signaling profoundly influences energy metabolism, adipokine release, and glucose homeostasis. It remains unclear whether altering these peripheral metabolic parameters inevitably feeds back to central circuits regulating mood, appetite, and reward, potentially undermining the 'peripheral restriction' strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sustained pharmacological restriction of peripheral CB1 receptors will induce significant alterations in circulating metabolic signals (e.g., leptin, ghrelin, glucose, specific lipids), which will in turn modulate neuronal activity and gene expression in key central circuits, such as the hypothalamic arcuate nucleus and the mesolimbic dopamine system.
proposals.method: We will employ a peripherally restricted CB1 inverse agonist (e.g., JD5037) in diet-induced obese mice. Central and peripheral effects will be measured in parallel. We will use in vivo fiber photometry to record calcium dynamics in genetically defined AgRP/POMC neurons in the arcuate nucleus and VTA dopamine neurons. This will be correlated with comprehensive metabolic phenotyping (plasma metabolomics/lipidomics, hormone panels). To establish causality, we will use chemogenetic inhibition (DREADDs) of specific hypothalamic nuclei during JD5037 treatment to see if blocking central integration of metabolic signals prevents central circuit adaptations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, mechanistic assessment of whether 'peripheral' CB1 modulation is truly separable from central effects. It will identify specific metabolic mediators responsible for any observed brain communication. The findings will critically inform the development of next-generation CB1-based therapeutics by defining the limits and necessary refinements of the peripheral restriction approach.
proposals.novelty: While prior work shows peripheral CB1 drugs affect metabolism and behavior, this study uniquely combines real-time, cell-type-specific neural circuit monitoring with deep metabolic profiling to directly trace the causal pathway from peripheral receptor blockade to central neuronal adaptation, moving beyond correlative observations.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Metabolic Role of Peripheral CB1 Receptors: A Tissue-Specific Knockout Study in Liver, Adipose, and Skeletal Muscle
proposals.motivation: The endocannabinoid system, particularly the CB1 receptor, is a well-established regulator of energy metabolism and a therapeutic target for metabolic syndrome. However, systemic CB1 antagonism, while effective, was halted due to neuropsychiatric side effects, highlighting the need to understand the specific metabolic contributions of peripheral CB1 receptors. Pre-2026 evidence suggests tissue-specific roles, but the relative importance of CB1 in key metabolic organs like liver, adipose, and muscle for whole-body homeostasis remains incompletely resolved.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CB1 receptors in liver, adipose tissue, and skeletal muscle have distinct and non-redundant roles in regulating systemic glucose homeostasis, lipid metabolism, and insulin sensitivity.
proposals.method: We will employ Cre-lox technology to generate and characterize three distinct tissue-specific CB1 knockout mouse lines: Alb-Cre (hepatocytes), Adipoq-Cre (adipocytes), and HSA-Cre (skeletal muscle). These models will be subjected to comprehensive metabolic phenotyping under both chow and high-fat diet conditions. We will measure body weight, adiposity, glucose tolerance (IPGTT), insulin tolerance (ITT), and circulating lipid and endocannabinoid levels. Tissue-specific molecular analyses (e.g., Western blot for insulin signaling proteins, qPCR for metabolic genes) will link the phenotypic observations to altered signaling pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively map the metabolic functions of peripheral CB1 receptors to specific tissues, identifying which organ's CB1 signaling is most critical for diet-induced metabolic dysregulation. The findings will provide a refined blueprint for developing next-generation, peripherally restricted CB1 modulators with improved safety profiles for treating obesity and type 2 diabetes.
proposals.novelty: While global and neuron-specific CB1 knockouts exist, a direct, parallel comparison of metabolic phenotypes from single-tissue deletions in liver, fat, and muscle within a unified experimental framework has not been performed, allowing for an unambiguous assessment of their individual contributions.

proposals.title: Targeting the Endocannabinoid System with a Dual CB1 Agonist/TRPV1 Antagonist to Counteract Muscle Wasting and Anorexia in a Preclinical Model of Cancer Cachexia
proposals.motivation: Cachexia, a debilitating wasting syndrome in cancer and AIDS, lacks effective treatments and significantly reduces patient survival and quality of life. Pre-2026 evidence shows that CB1 receptor activation can stimulate appetite and anabolism, but monotherapies like dronabinol have shown limited efficacy on muscle mass, potentially due to compensatory or opposing pathways such as TRPV1 signaling which can promote catabolism.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single molecule acting as a CB1 receptor agonist and TRPV1 channel antagonist will synergistically improve both hyperphagia and skeletal muscle anabolism, thereby more effectively reversing the dual metabolic and nutritional deficits of cachexia than selective CB1 agonism alone.
proposals.method: We will employ an in silico-guided drug design approach, based on our group's expertise with 4-(thiophen-2-yl)butanamides, to develop and synthesize novel dual-target ligands. Candidate efficacy will be tested in a murine model of Lewis lung carcinoma-induced cachexia. We will measure food intake, body composition (EchoMRI), and grip strength. Ex vivo, we will analyze signaling pathways (Akt/mTOR, ubiquitin-proteasome) in skeletal muscle and assess CB1 receptor expression and function using techniques established in our statin-induced myotoxicity model to identify any cachexia-induced ECS dysfunction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide proof-of-concept for a novel polypharmacology strategy within the ECS to treat cachexia. It will generate a new chemical tool to dissect the crosstalk between CB1 and TRPV1 in energy homeostasis and muscle metabolism, moving the field beyond appetite stimulation alone.
proposals.novelty: While CB1 agonists for cachexia have been explored, the rational design of a single dual-modality agent targeting both the orexigenic CB1 and the catabolic TRPV1 to create a synergistic anabolic effect is a novel and untested therapeutic strategy.

proposals.title: Mechanistic dissection of cannabidiol-induced tumor cell death: a quantitative analysis of autophagic flux and regulated necrosis in cannabinoid receptor-dependent and -independent models
proposals.motivation: While CBD's antitumor effects are documented, the predominant focus on apoptosis overlooks other critical death pathways. Pre-2026 evidence suggests cannabinoids can modulate autophagy and interact with TRP channels, but a systematic, quantitative analysis of non-apoptotic death mechanisms triggered by CBD in specific tumor contexts is lacking. This gap limits our understanding of resistance mechanisms and the full therapeutic potential of cannabinoid-based oncology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in specific tumor cell lines resistant to canonical apoptotic signaling, CBD exerts its primary cytotoxic effect by inducing excessive autophagy or specific forms of regulated necrosis (e.g., necroptosis), and that these pathways are differentially engaged depending on cannabinoid receptor (CB1/CB2) and TRPV1 expression and activity.
proposals.method: We will employ isogenic human tumor cell lines (e.g., glioma, breast cancer) with CRISPR/Cas9-mediated knockout of CB1, CB2, or TRPV1 receptors to isolate receptor-specific effects. Cell death will be quantified via high-content imaging using fluorescent probes for apoptosis (Annexin V/7-AAD), autophagy (LC3-II puncta formation via mRFP-GFP-LC3 tandem reporter), and necroptosis (phospho-MLKL). Pharmacological inhibitors (e.g., chloroquine for autophagy, Nec-1 for necroptosis) and real-time metabolic flux analysis (Seahorse) will delineate causal pathways. Data will be integrated using multivariate analysis to define death mechanism signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, mechanistic map of non-apoptotic death pathways activated by CBD, clarifying the roles of specific cannabinoid and TRP receptors. It will identify potential biomarkers (receptor expression profiles) for predicting tumor cell sensitivity to CBD, informing future combinatorial therapy strategies that target alternative death pathways to overcome apoptosis resistance.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond the correlative assessment of cell death to causally dissect and quantify the contribution of autophagy and necrosis in CBD's action, using precise genetic and pharmacological tools within Dr. Di Marzo's expertise on cannabinoid and TRPV1 signaling, an approach not comprehensively applied to CBD's antitumor mechanisms pre-2026.

proposals.title: Dissecting the indirect mechanisms of cannabidiol (CBD) anti-tumor efficacy: A focus on host immune modulation and angiogenesis in a syngeneic mouse model of melanoma.
proposals.motivation: Preclinical studies before 2026 show that cannabidiol (CBD) exhibits anti-tumor effects, but its mechanisms remain debated. While CBD can act on tumor cells directly via TRPV1 or other targets, its complex immunomodulatory and anti-angiogenic properties, often mediated through the endocannabinoid system, suggest potential indirect host-mediated mechanisms. Determining if CBD's primary in vivo efficacy requires direct tumor cell targeting is crucial for rational therapeutic design and understanding its true mode of action.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the in vivo anti-tumor efficacy of CBD in a syngeneic melanoma model is mediated predominantly through indirect mechanisms, specifically the modulation of host immune cell infiltration/function and the inhibition of tumor angiogenesis, rather than through direct cytotoxicity on cancer cells.
proposals.method: We will employ B16-F10 melanoma cells implanted subcutaneously in immunocompetent C57BL/6 mice and immunodeficient NSG mice. Mice will be treated with CBD. Tumor growth will be monitored. We will use flow cytometry to quantify tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs), myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs), and tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) from digested tumors. Angiogenesis will be assessed via immunohistochemistry for CD31+ microvessel density and by measuring levels of VEGF and other angiogenic factors in tumor homogenates using ELISA. Ex vivo co-culture experiments of CBD-treated splenocytes with B16-F10 cells will assess immune-mediated cytotoxicity.
proposals.contribution: This work will delineate the relative contribution of immune and vascular host systems to CBD's anti-tumor activity. It will provide a mechanistic framework for understanding CBD's complex pharmacology in oncology, potentially identifying biomarkers (specific immune cell populations or angiogenic factors) for its efficacy. The findings could redirect therapeutic strategies towards enhancing these indirect host-mediated effects.
proposals.novelty: While CBD's direct effects on cancer cells are documented, systematically isolating and proving the sufficiency of indirect host-mediated mechanisms in a controlled in vivo setting, using comparative models of immunocompetent and immunodeficient mice, is a novel and critical test of its primary mode of action.

proposals.title: Systematic Profiling of Cannabidiol's Cytotoxic Mechanisms Reveals Context-Dependent Signaling in Diverse Cancer Cell Lines
proposals.motivation: Cannabidiol (CBD) exhibits anti-proliferative effects in various cancer models, but its proposed mechanisms are disparate, involving TRPV1 activation, oxidative stress induction, and modulation of endocannabinoid tone. This lack of a unified molecular signature, coupled with CBD's known promiscuity for multiple receptors, suggests its effects may be highly cell-context dependent. A systematic, multi-parametric investigation across a broad panel of cell types is needed to clarify whether CBD possesses a unique, conserved mode of action or acts as a multi-target agent with pleiotropic outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cannabidiol (CBD) does not act through a single, conserved primary mechanism across different cancer cell types, but rather that its cytotoxic and anti-proliferative effects are mediated by distinct, cell-type-specific signaling pathways involving differential engagement of cannabinoid receptors (CB1, CB2), TRPV1, and other molecular targets.
proposals.method: We will employ a panel of 10-12 human cancer cell lines representing diverse tissues (e.g., breast MDA-MB-231, prostate PC-3, glioblastoma U87-MG, colon HCT116). Cytotoxicity (MTT assay) and apoptosis (caspase-3/7 activation) will be measured following CBD treatment. To dissect mechanisms, we will use selective pharmacological antagonists (e.g., AM251 for CB1, AM630 for CB2, capsazepine for TRPV1) and siRNA-mediated knockdown of target genes (CNR1, CNR2, TRPV1) prior to CBD exposure. Key signaling readouts (phospho-ERK, p-Akt, intracellular Ca2+ flux, reactive oxygen species) will be quantified. Data will be analyzed via multivariate analysis to cluster cell lines by their dominant CBD-response signature.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive, comparative map of CBD's primary molecular targets and downstream signaling events across a spectrum of cancers. It will clarify the controversial role of the endocannabinoid system in CBD's anti-cancer effects and identify which cancer types are most likely to respond to CBD via specific, targetable pathways. The findings will inform the rational design of combination therapies and biomarker selection for future preclinical studies.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies examine CBD in isolated cell models, this systematic, side-by-side pharmacological and genetic dissection across a broad panel, integrating the researcher's expertise in cannabinoid and TRPV1 receptor signaling, is novel. It directly tests the fundamental question of mechanistic conservation versus context-dependency, moving beyond single-pathway claims.

proposals.title: Metabolomic Profiling of Cannabidiol-Induced Metabolic Rewiring in Cancer and Normal Cells: A Systems-Level Approach to Uncover Selective Mechanisms
proposals.motivation: Cannabidiol (CBD) exhibits anti-cancer properties in vitro and in vivo, but its mechanisms are complex and involve multiple targets beyond cannabinoid receptors, including TRP channels and metabolic enzymes. Pre-2026 research has largely focused on specific signaling pathways, leaving a critical gap in understanding the global metabolic consequences of CBD exposure. A comprehensive, unbiased metabolomic analysis is essential to map the metabolic vulnerabilities of cancer cells versus the resilience of normal cells, which could reveal novel therapeutic targets and biomarkers for CBD-based interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CBD induces distinct, cell-type-specific metabolic alterations, and that cancer cells will exhibit a unique metabolic signature of dysregulation (e.g., in lipid metabolism, bioenergetics, and redox balance) compared to normal cells, which underlies their selective sensitivity.
proposals.method: We will employ global, untargeted metabolomics using liquid chromatography coupled to high-resolution mass spectrometry (LC-HRMS) on human breast cancer (MDA-MB-231) and non-tumorigenic mammary epithelial (MCF-10A) cell lines treated with physiologically relevant doses of CBD. Data will be processed using XCMS Online for peak picking, alignment, and annotation against pre-2026 databases (e.g., HMDB, METLIN). Multivariate statistical analysis (PCA, PLS-DA) and pathway enrichment analysis (via MetaboAnalyst 5.0) will identify significantly altered metabolites and pathways. Key findings will be validated using targeted metabolomics and functional assays measuring mitochondrial respiration (Seahorse) and lipid droplet formation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive map of CBD-induced metabolic changes across cell types, directly linking CBD's pleiotropic actions to specific metabolic nodes. It will identify candidate metabolic pathways that confer selective toxicity to cancer cells, offering new mechanistic insights for CBD's anti-cancer effects. The data will serve as a foundational resource for developing metabolic biomarkers and rational combination therapies.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Di Marzo's prior work has characterized receptor-level signaling, this proposal applies a systems metabolomics approach—a method not yet extensively used in his CBD/cancer research—to move beyond single targets and reveal the integrated metabolic network response, which is non-obvious from studying isolated pathways.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Transcriptional Profiling Reveals Heterogeneous Endocannabinoid System Remodeling and Survival Signaling in Tumor Cells Treated with Cannabidiol (CBD)
proposals.motivation: Cannabidiol (CBD) exhibits complex, often contradictory anti-tumor effects in vitro, with studies reporting both pro-apoptotic and pro-survival outcomes. This variability is poorly understood but may stem from heterogeneous cellular responses within a tumor population, a dimension obscured by bulk analyses. Characterizing this heterogeneity is crucial for predicting therapeutic efficacy and resistance mechanisms of cannabinoid-based therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exposure to CBD induces distinct, transcriptionally defined subpopulations within a clonal tumor cell line, characterized by divergent regulation of endocannabinoid system (ECS) components (e.g., CNR1, CNR2, TRPV1, FAAH) and activation of competing pro-death and pro-survival signaling pathways.
proposals.method: We will treat a human glioblastoma cell line (U87-MG) with a physiologically relevant dose of CBD (10 µM) for 24 hours. Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) will be performed using the 10x Genomics Chromium platform. Data analysis will involve standard pipelines (Cell Ranger, Seurat) for quality control, clustering, and differential expression. We will specifically employ trajectory inference algorithms (e.g., Monocle3, PAGA) to reconstruct potential cell-state transitions and identify gene regulatory networks driving heterogeneity in ECS and stress-response pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution map of single-cell transcriptional heterogeneity in response to CBD in cancer cells. It will identify novel biomarkers for CBD-sensitive versus resistant cell states and delineate the co-regulated pathways that define these states, offering mechanistic insights into the variable efficacy of cannabinoids.
proposals.novelty: While bulk studies of CBD on ECS in cancer exist, applying scRNA-seq to dissect the heterogeneous transcriptional response is novel. This approach moves beyond measuring average 'levels' of receptors and signaling to defining the functional ECS states that coexist within a treated population, linking them to fate decisions.

proposals.title: Characterization of Acquired Cannabidiol Resistance in Breast Cancer: Role of Endocannabinoid System Remodeling and Metabolic Reprogramming
proposals.motivation: Cannabidiol (CBD) exhibits promising anti-cancer properties in pre-clinical models, but the potential for tumors to develop resistance, a common limitation of chemotherapeutics, remains unexplored. Understanding the mechanisms of acquired CBD resistance is critical for predicting clinical efficacy and designing rational combination therapies. This investigation addresses a fundamental gap in cannabinoid pharmacology and cancer biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that prolonged exposure of breast cancer cells to CBD selects for resistant phenotypes driven by specific adaptations in endocannabinoid system components (e.g., CB1/TRPV1 receptor expression/signaling) and associated metabolic pathways, such as lipid metabolism and oxidative stress response.
proposals.method: We will generate CBD-resistant MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7 cell lines via chronic, stepwise exposure to increasing CBD concentrations. Resistant clones will be characterized for viability (MTT), apoptosis (Annexin V), and proliferation. We will profile mRNA (qPCR) and protein (Western blot) levels of CB1, CB2, TRPV1, and FAAH. Signaling will be assessed via calcium imaging and ERK/p38 phosphorylation. Metabolic adaptations will be analyzed using Seahorse extracellular flux analysis and lipidomic profiling.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first in vitro model of acquired CBD resistance in breast cancer, identifying key molecular drivers of this phenotype. It will provide a mechanistic framework linking endocannabinoid system dysregulation to cancer cell survival under cannabinoid pressure, informing future biomarker and combination therapy development.
proposals.novelty: While most pre-2026 research focuses on CBD's efficacy, this proposal uniquely investigates the 'inverse problem' of resistance. It applies the established pharmacological concept of acquired resistance to a non-psychoactive phytocannabinoid, specifically probing adaptations in the endocannabinoid and related systems.

proposals.title: Context-Dependent Switch in THC's Convulsant Profile: Role of Endocannabinoid Tone and CB1 Receptor Signaling in Epileptogenic Networks
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 literature presents a paradox: Δ9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) can be proconvulsant in healthy rodents but anticonvulsant in certain pathological models. This unresolved contradiction limits therapeutic application and reflects a fundamental gap in understanding how disease states alter endocannabinoid system (ECS) function. Resolving this is critical for predicting cannabinoid efficacy and safety in neurological disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pathological reduction in endocannabinoid 'tone' and/or a compensatory upregulation of CB1 receptor signaling in specific neuronal circuits converts THC's action from a net excitatory (proconvulsant) to a net inhibitory (anticonvulsant) effect.
proposals.method: We will use a multi-level approach in mice: 1) Induce chronic epilepsy via intrahippocampal kainic acid and characterize ECS changes (AEA, 2-AG levels, CB1 receptor expression/function) in hippocampal subregions vs. healthy controls. 2) Employ ex vivo electrophysiology on brain slices to test THC's effects on synaptic transmission and network excitability in healthy vs. epileptic tissue, using selective CB1 antagonists to isolate mechanisms. 3) Conduct in vivo video-EEG monitoring to correlate THC's dose-dependent effects on seizure frequency and severity with the measured ECS alterations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic framework explaining THC's dual convulsant/anticonvulsant actions, directly linking its effects to the underlying ECS pathology. It will identify specific biomarkers (e.g., regional 2-AG levels, CB1 receptor coupling efficiency) that predict THC's therapeutic window, moving beyond trial-and-error towards precision medicine in cannabinoid-based epilepsy treatment.
proposals.novelty: This study systematically inverts the default assumption of THC as a uniform proconvulsant by testing the novel premise that disease-induced ECS plasticity is the critical determinant of its net effect on neuronal excitability, a hypothesis not directly addressed in pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Cannabidiol's Therapeutic Efficacy in a Neuroinflammatory Model Requires Concurrent Modulation of TRPV1 and Adenosine A2A Receptors
proposals.motivation: Cannabidiol (CBD) is a promising therapeutic for neuroinflammatory and neurological disorders, but its mechanism of action remains controversial. Pre-2026 research has proposed numerous single targets (e.g., CB1, TRPV1, A2A, 5-HT1A), yet pharmacological blockade of any one often fails to fully abolish CBD's effects, suggesting a polypharmacological basis for its efficacy that is not systematically tested.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CBD's in vivo efficacy in a neuroinflammatory model is not mediated by a single primary target but requires the simultaneous engagement of at least two distinct receptor systems, specifically TRPV1 and adenosine A2A receptors.
proposals.method: We will use a well-established mouse model of LPS-induced neuroinflammation, assessing CBD's effects on cytokine levels, microglial activation, and pain hypersensitivity. Using a selective pharmacological antagonism approach, we will sequentially and combinatorially block candidate receptors (TRPV1, A2A, CB1, 5-HT1A, GPR55) with antagonists like capsazepine (TRPV1), SCH58261 (A2A), and AM251 (CB1). Behavioral and biochemical readouts will be analyzed using multi-factorial ANOVA to identify significant interactions between blocked pathways, indicating a requirement for multi-target engagement.
