proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct experimental evidence for or against the polypharmacological hypothesis of CBD action, moving the field beyond the search for a single primary target. It will identify a minimal set of concurrent receptor modulations necessary for efficacy, informing more rational design of multi-target therapeutics inspired by CBD's profile.
proposals.novelty: While multi-target actions of CBD are often cited, this study is novel in systematically testing the necessity of simultaneous engagement of specific receptor combinations in a relevant disease model, rather than merely cataloging CBD's affinities or observing additive effects of single-target drugs.

proposals.title: From Historical Texts to Testable Hypotheses: A Systematic Deconstruction of Ancient Cannabis Claims to Identify Novel Endocannabinoid System Modulation Pathways
proposals.motivation: The widespread historical and ethnobotanical use of *Cannabis sativa* is often cited as de facto evidence for its therapeutic potential, but this conflates cultural practice with pharmacological validity. A rigorous, critical analysis separating myth from biologically plausible mechanisms is lacking. This gap hinders the efficient translation of historical leads into testable hypotheses for modern drug discovery targeting the endocannabinoid system (ECS).
proposals.hypothesis: A systematic, critical evaluation of pre-modern cannabis use reports will identify a subset of historical indications with a high prior probability of efficacy, which will be linked to specific, testable modes of action on the ECS and related lipid signaling pathways (e.g., TRP channels).
proposals.method: We will first conduct a systematic review of historical texts (e.g., classical, medieval, early modern pharmacopoeias) and ethnobotanical records, cataloguing claimed uses and administration methods. Using a pre-defined pharmacological plausibility filter (considering known cannabinoid receptor distribution, pharmacokinetics, and bioactive compound presence), we will score and rank these indications. High-probability targets (e.g., neuropathic pain, spasticity) will then be modeled *in vitro* using murine primary neuronal cultures or induced neuronal models to assess ECS signaling (CB1/CB2 receptor expression, 2-AG/AEA levels) and TRPV1 activity in response to specific cannabis phytocannabinoid profiles matching historical preparations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formalized, critical framework for evaluating historical drug claims, moving beyond anecdote. It will generate a shortlist of high-priority, pharmacologically-grounded therapeutic hypotheses for cannabis-based medicines, directly informing future preclinical and clinical research on ECS modulation.
proposals.novelty: While historical reviews of cannabis exist, this proposal uniquely integrates philological analysis with contemporary neuropharmacological filters and direct *in vitro* testing to validate the transition from text to testable mechanism, a approach not previously applied systematically to the ECS field.

proposals.title: Quantitative EEG Biomarkers as Translational Tools to Characterize the Central Pharmacodynamics of Cannabidiol in a Mouse Model of Neuroinflammation
proposals.motivation: Cannabidiol (CBD) shows therapeutic promise for CNS disorders, but its central pharmacodynamic effects are poorly quantified, relying on subjective or invasive measures. Quantitative EEG (qEEG) provides objective, translatable biomarkers of drug action on neural circuits, successfully used in development of antidepressants and antipsychotics. Applying this established neurophysiological framework to CBD would objectively map its CNS engagement, bridging molecular mechanisms with functional neural readouts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CBD administration will produce a distinct, dose-dependent qEEG signature in mice, characterized by increased theta power and decreased beta/gamma power, correlating with its anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective effects in a lipopolysaccharide-induced neuroinflammation model.
proposals.method: We will record cortical EEG in C57BL/6J mice implanted with telemetric transmitters. Following acute CBD (0, 10, 30, 100 mg/kg i.p.), we will compute established qEEG biomarkers: absolute/relative band power (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma), and connectivity measures (coherence, phase-lag index). Analysis will use Welch's method for power spectral density and custom MATLAB scripts for network metrics. These signatures will be correlated with plasma CBD/endocannabinoid levels (LC-MS/MS) and hippocampal cytokine expression (ELISA).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish an objective, translational qEEG pharmacodynamic signature for CBD, linking its central effects to systemic endocannabinoid tone and inflammatory markers. It will provide a non-invasive tool for dose optimization and mechanistic de-risking in future CBD-based CNS drug development.
proposals.novelty: While qEEG is used for classic psychoactives, its systematic application to map CBD's specific, subtle CNS effects is novel. Integrating this neurophysiological readout with Di Marzo's expertise in endocannabinoid level analysis creates a unique multi-modal pharmacodynamic profile.

proposals.title: A Systems Pharmacology Network Analysis of Cannabidiol's Polypharmacology in Epilepsy: Integrating Endocannabinoid, TRPV, and Adenosine Signaling
proposals.motivation: Cannabidiol (CBD) is an approved antiseizure drug, but its mechanism of action remains incompletely understood due to its complex polypharmacology, involving multiple targets beyond the canonical endocannabinoid system (e.g., TRPV1, adenosine A1 receptors). A holistic, network-based understanding is needed to explain its clinical efficacy and side-effect profile, which cannot be deduced from single-target studies. This knowledge gap limits the rational development of safer, more effective multi-target epilepsy therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CBD's antiseizure efficacy arises from its coordinated modulation of a specific, interconnected signaling network involving CB1 receptor desensitization, TRPV1 activation, and adenosine signaling, which collectively rebalance neuronal excitability in epileptic foci.
proposals.method: We will construct a quantitative systems pharmacology model using pre-2026 knowledge and tools. First, we will curate a protein-protein interaction and signaling network for epilepsy-relevant brain regions from public databases (STRING, KEGG). CBD's binding affinities (Ki) and functional data for primary targets (CB1, CB2, TRPV1, A1R, GPR55) from the literature will be integrated as node perturbations. Using the Cytoscape platform and algorithms like Influence Propagation, we will simulate the network's state transition from 'hyperexcitable' to 'balanced' upon CBD exposure. Key predictions (e.g., altered phosphorylation states of CB1 and specific adenosine transporter levels) will be validated in hippocampal neurons derived from induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) and in a mouse model of pentylenetetrazole-induced seizures, using western blot, calcium imaging, and microdialysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated, mechanistic network model of CBD's polypharmacology in epilepsy, moving beyond a list of targets to a dynamic systems-level understanding. It will identify critical nodes and synergistic pathways responsible for the therapeutic effect, offering a template for analyzing other plant-derived polypharmacological agents. The validated model will serve as a predictive tool for identifying novel drug combinations or off-targets contributing to adverse effects.
proposals.novelty: While CBD's individual targets are known, applying a pre-defined, quantitative systems pharmacology network analysis to deconvolve their integrated contribution to an antiseizure phenotype is novel. This approach uniquely combines Di Marzo's expertise in endocannabinoid and TRPV1 signaling with computational network propagation algorithms to explain emergent therapeutic properties.

proposals.title: Population-Level Analysis of Cannabis Use Patterns and Epilepsy Outcomes: An Epidemiological Study Leveraging the UK Biobank and CUDIT-R Data
proposals.motivation: Preclinical and clinical evidence pre-2026 indicates cannabinoids, particularly CBD, have antiseizure effects, but population-level data on the relationship between real-world cannabis use (including recreational) and epilepsy epidemiology is scarce. Understanding this link is crucial for public health, as widespread cannabis legalization may influence neurological disorder patterns, yet the impact of different use modalities (frequency, type, motivation) on epilepsy incidence and severity remains unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific patterns of cannabis use (e.g., high-frequency use, use for self-medication, or use of high-THC products) are associated with differential incidence rates and clinical severity profiles of epilepsy in the general population, independent of known confounding factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort analysis using the UK Biobank dataset (pre-2026 release), integrating deep phenotyping data with linked primary care and hospital records to identify epilepsy diagnoses and severity proxies (e.g., frequency of healthcare encounters, polypharmacy). Cannabis use patterns will be characterized using the Cannabis Use Disorders Identification Test-Revised (CUDIT-R) metrics and self-reported use data. We will employ multivariate Cox proportional hazards models for incidence and multinomial logistic regression for severity, adjusting for key confounders (socioeconomic status, other substance use, psychiatric comorbidities). Machine learning techniques, specifically random forest classification, will be used to identify complex interaction patterns between use variables and outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, epidemiological evidence directly linking detailed cannabis use behaviors to epilepsy at a population level. It will move beyond clinical trials of purified CBD to inform public health strategies regarding cannabis policy and generate hypotheses about the role of the endocannabinoid system in epileptogenesis in naturalistic settings.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Di Marzo's prior work focuses on molecular and cellular mechanisms, this proposal applies his expertise in endocannabinoid signaling to a novel, translational epidemiological context, using a pre-2026 dataset of unprecedented scale and depth to dissect real-world cannabis use patterns rather than administered pharmaceutical agents.

proposals.title: A Systems Ecology Framework for Predicting Network Stability Perturbations by Cannabinoid Polypharmacology
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the therapeutic promise of cannabinoids is tempered by complex side effects, often attributed to their polypharmacology across CB1, CB2, TRPV1, and other targets. Current in silico polypharmacology models predict binding profiles but fail to forecast the systemic, emergent consequences of multi-target engagement on cellular signaling networks. This gap hinders the rational design of safer, multi-target cannabinoid ligands.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the systemic impact of a cannabinoid ligand on cellular signaling can be predicted by modeling its target profile as a perturbation to a protein interaction network, where ecological stability metrics correlate with experimentally observed functional resilience or adverse effect risk.
proposals.method: We will construct a directed human protein-protein interaction network focused on endocannabinoid, inflammatory, and metabolic pathways from pre-2026 databases (STRING, KEGG). Using polypharmacology models (e.g., SEA, SwissTargetPrediction), we will generate perturbation vectors for known cannabinoids. We will adapt the Allometric Trophic Network (ATN) model and use generalized Lotka-Volterra dynamics to simulate network stability. Key outputs will be ecological metrics: robustness (proportion of nodes maintaining function post-perturbation), resilience (rate of return to equilibrium), and cascade failure risk.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework to move beyond single-target affinity to a systems-level forecast of ligand effects. It will generate testable predictions for ligand-induced network instability, correlating metrics with in vivo adverse effect data. This contributes a new paradigm for evaluating the therapeutic window of polypharmacological agents.
proposals.novelty: The direct adaptation of quantitative ecological network stability theory to pharmacological network perturbation is novel. While systems biology models exist, applying metrics like cascade failure risk from food web ecology to cannabinoid signaling represents a significant conceptual and methodological crossover.

proposals.title: In Silico Prediction and Design of Cannabinoid Allosteric Modulators with Tailored Polypharmacology Profiles
proposals.motivation: The therapeutic potential of cannabinoids is limited by on-target adverse effects from orthosteric CB1 agonism and complex polypharmacology that is poorly understood. Pre-2026 research indicates cannabinoids can allosterically modulate non-canonical GPCRs (e.g., 5-HT, adenosine), but predicting these interactions is a major challenge. A computational platform to rationally design ligands with a pre-defined allosteric polypharmacology profile would enable the development of safer, more effective therapeutics by sculpting signaling across receptor networks.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models trained on structural and pharmacological data can accurately predict the allosteric effects of cannabinoid chemotypes across a network of GPCRs, enabling the in silico design of novel ligands with a desired multi-target signaling fingerprint.
proposals.method: We will integrate structure-based modeling (molecular docking and molecular dynamics simulations using GPCR structures from the PDB) with ligand-based machine learning (using graph neural networks on chemical structures). A multi-task neural network architecture will be trained on pre-2026 public and proprietary data (e.g., from ChEMBL, BindingDB) to predict allosteric modulation direction and potency at CB1, CB2, and a panel of phylogenetically related GPCRs (5-HT1A, A2A, μ-opioid). The trained model will guide the de novo design of novel 4-(thiophen-2-yl)butanamide analogs using a generative deep learning model (e.g., a variational autoencoder) coupled with activity prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel computational platform for predicting cannabinoid allosteric polypharmacology, a significant advance over single-target or orthosteric-focused models. It will generate a series of in silico-designed lead compounds with predicted favorable multi-target profiles, providing testable hypotheses for experimental validation and a new strategy to mitigate adverse effects in cannabinoid-based drug discovery.
proposals.novelty: This approach uniquely fuses the prediction of allosteric (not orthosteric) interactions with polypharmacology modeling specifically for cannabinoid scaffolds. It moves beyond retrospective analysis to the generative design of new chemotypes with a pre-specified allosteric signaling fingerprint, a non-obvious integration of concepts from pre-2026 computational pharmacology.

proposals.title: Computational Polypharmacology Profiling of Cannabinoid Ligands to Predict Off-Target Interactions with Nuclear Receptors and Ion Channels
proposals.motivation: The therapeutic potential of cannabinoid ligands is often limited by off-target effects and complex pharmacology beyond CB1/CB2 receptors, including interactions with TRP channels and nuclear receptors like PPARγ. Pre-2026, the rational design of selective ligands remains challenging due to a lack of comprehensive in silico tools to predict multi-target profiles. This project addresses the need for predictive models to de-risk drug development by forecasting adverse or synergistic polypharmacology early in the discovery pipeline.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that established cannabinoid scaffolds, including those targeting CB1, CB2, and TRPV1, possess predictable off-target affinities for a defined set of nuclear receptors and ion channels, which can be accurately forecasted using structure-based and ligand-based polypharmacology models.
proposals.method: We will employ an integrated computational workflow. First, we will curate a dataset of known cannabinoid ligands and their activity profiles from ChEMBL and GtoPdb. Structure-based predictions will be performed using molecular docking with AutoDock Vina and GLIDE against homology models or crystal structures of primary (CB1, CB2, TRPV1) and secondary targets (PPARγ, GPR55, TRPA1). Ligand-based predictions will utilize similarity ensemble approach (SEA) and machine learning models (Random Forest, SVM) trained on chemical fingerprints to extrapolate off-target probabilities. The consensus predictions will be validated against existing in-house biological data from neuronal and muscle cell assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a publicly accessible computational framework for the polypharmacological screening of cannabinoid compounds. It will provide specific predictions for off-target liabilities of key scaffolds, guiding the synthesis of safer ligands. The results will advance the mechanistic understanding of cannabinoid signaling complexity in physiological systems like neurons and skeletal muscle.
proposals.novelty: While polypharmacology modeling exists, its dedicated application to systematically map the off-target landscape of cannabinoid ligands beyond the canonical endocannabinoid system is novel. The integration of in silico predictions with Di Marzo's group expertise in cannabinoid receptor signaling and TRPV1 agonist design creates a unique translational feedback loop.

proposals.title: High-Throughput, Multiplexed Kinetic Profiling of Cannabinoid Receptor Signaling Using a FRET-Biosensor Array Platform
proposals.motivation: The functional characterization of cannabinoid ligands is hindered by low-throughput, single-receptor assays that fail to capture the complex, real-time signaling kinetics across the endocannabinoid system (ECS). This bottleneck limits the discovery of selective ligands and the understanding of ligand-biased signaling, crucial for developing safer therapeutics targeting CB1, CB2, and GPR55 receptors. A platform enabling parallel, comparative kinetic analysis is urgently needed to accelerate pharmacological research in this field.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting high-throughput FRET-biosensor arrays to simultaneously monitor multiple cannabinoid receptor signaling pathways will reveal unique, ligand-specific kinetic signatures and enable the rapid identification of receptor-selective and signaling-biased compounds.
proposals.method: We will engineer stable HEK293 cell lines, each expressing a distinct, validated FRET biosensor for cAMP (for CB1, CB2) or Ca2+ (for GPR55) downstream of a specific cannabinoid receptor (CB1, CB2, GPR55). These cell lines will be arrayed in a 96-well plate format, with each well type dedicated to one receptor-biosensor pair. Using a high-speed, multi-wavelength plate reader (e.g., a modified FLIPR or similar system), we will perform simultaneous, real-time FRET ratio imaging across all wells upon automated addition of a library of cannabinoid ligands (e.g., phytocannabinoids, synthetic agonists/antagonists, endocannabinoids). Kinetic parameters (onset, amplitude, duration) will be extracted via custom analysis scripts in Python/Matlab.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first integrated platform for the multiplexed, kinetic profiling of the ECS, generating unprecedented datasets on ligand-receptor interaction dynamics. It will directly contribute to the GtoPdb database with quantitative kinetic parameters and will identify novel ligand properties, such as kinetic selectivity and biased signaling, informing future drug design for neurological and metabolic disorders.
proposals.novelty: While FRET biosensors and plate readers exist, their application to create a dedicated, multiplexed array for parallel real-time kinetic comparison of all major cannabinoid receptor signaling pathways is novel. This approach transcends traditional endpoint or single-pathway assays by directly adapting high-throughput electrophysiology concepts to optical biosensor technology for the ECS.

proposals.title: A Whole-Organism Spatial Atlas of Cannabinoid Receptor Expression and Functional Signaling in the Mouse
proposals.motivation: The endocannabinoid system (ECS) is a ubiquitous neuromodulatory network involved in a vast array of physiological and pathological processes. Current knowledge of ECS component distribution is fragmented, relying on tissue-specific studies of single receptors or enzymes. A comprehensive, organism-wide map of cannabinoid target expression and activity is critically lacking, hindering our understanding of systemic ECS function and off-target effects of cannabinoid-based therapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cannabinoid receptors (CB1, CB2, TRPV1) and key metabolic enzymes (FAAH, MAGL) exhibit distinct, organ-specific spatial expression patterns that correlate with regional differences in endocannabinoid tone and signaling responsiveness in vivo.
proposals.method: We will employ tissue-clearing (e.g., CLARITY) combined with multiplexed fluorescent in situ hybridization (FISH) and immunohistochemistry on whole adult C57BL/6J mice to create a 3D spatial atlas of ECS component mRNA and protein. Functional mapping will be achieved by administering a cannabinoid receptor agonist (e.g., WIN55,212-2) and quantifying region-specific phosphorylation of downstream effectors (e.g., ERK1/2) via whole-mount light-sheet microscopy and automated image analysis pipelines (e.g., using Ilastik and ClearMap2).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated, high-resolution spatial and functional map of the ECS across a mammalian organism. It will serve as a foundational resource for predicting organ-specific drug effects, identifying novel physiological roles for the ECS, and understanding the systemic basis of ECS-related pathologies.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies focus on the brain or isolated tissues, this proposal leverages advanced tissue-clearing and 3D imaging to map the entire ECS—receptors and functional signaling—simultaneously across all major organs, moving from a reductionist to a holistic systems-level analysis.

proposals.title: Inverse Multi-Target Design of Selective Cannabinoid Receptor Ligands via Constrained Multi-Objective Optimization
proposals.motivation: The therapeutic potential of cannabinoid receptor ligands is limited by the challenge of achieving precise polypharmacological profiles, such as high CB2 agonism for anti-inflammation with minimal CB1 or off-target (e.g., 5-HT2A, adrenergic) activity to avoid psychoactive and cardiovascular side effects. Current ligand discovery is largely empirical and sequential, failing to systematically navigate the chemical space against multiple, often conflicting, receptor activation objectives simultaneously.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing ligand design as a constrained multi-objective optimization problem, we can inversely engineer novel synthetic cannabinoids that satisfy a pre-defined, optimal activation profile across CB1, CB2, and key off-target receptors.
proposals.method: We will define a ligand chemical space based on known cannabinoid scaffolds (e.g., 4-(thiophen-2-yl)butanamides). Using pre-2026 in silico methods (molecular docking, quantitative structure-activity relationship models, and pre-trained deep learning architectures like graph neural networks), we will build predictive models for efficacy (Emax) and potency (pEC50) at CB1, CB2, TRPV1, 5-HT2A, and α-adrenergic receptors. These models will serve as objective functions for a multi-objective optimization algorithm (e.g., NSGA-II) to search for Pareto-optimal compounds under constraints of drug-likeness (Lipinski's rules). Top computational candidates will be synthesized and pharmacologically characterized in vitro using cell-based assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational framework for the rational, multi-target inverse design of cannabinoid ligands and a set of new chemical entities with validated, tailored receptor profiles. It will provide a blueprint for designing safer, more effective cannabinoid-based therapeutics with minimized side-effect liabilities.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in adapting formal multi-objective optimization strategies from engineering disciplines to the inverse design of multi-target cannabinoid ligands, moving beyond single-target optimization or simple filtering to actively balance multiple, competing pharmacological objectives.

proposals.title: Mechanistic dissection of endovanilloid-TRPV1 interactions: distinguishing direct agonism from indirect modulation
proposals.motivation: The TRPV1 channel is a critical integrator of noxious stimuli, and its activation by endogenous lipids (endovanilloids) is implicated in pain and inflammation. Pre-2026 literature proposes several lipids, such as N-arachidonoyl-dopamine (NADA) and certain lipoxygenase products, as direct agonists, but evidence is often correlative. A systematic, comparative investigation is needed to clarify if all proposed endovanilloids act directly or require cellular co-factors, which is essential for understanding TRPV1 physiology and for rational drug design targeting this pathway.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that not all proposed endovanilloid lipids are direct TRPV1 agonists; a subset may require indirect mechanisms or specific cellular/molecular co-factors for channel activation.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-tiered approach using HEK293 cells stably expressing human TRPV1 and primary sensory neurons from wild-type and TRPV1-knockout mice. First, calcium imaging and patch-clamp electrophysiology will test a panel of endovanilloids (e.g., NADA, 12-HPETE, anandamide) on recombinant TRPV1 in HEK293 cells to establish direct agonism. Second, we will compare responses in neurons, assessing sensitivity to pharmacological inhibitors of downstream signaling (e.g., PLC, PKC) and using lipidomics (LC-MS/MS) to profile endogenous lipid changes upon stimulation. Third, in silico molecular docking (using pre-2026 structures like PDB 5IRX) will predict binding modes and affinities for candidate lipids.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, mechanistic classification of endovanilloids, resolving ambiguities in the literature. It will identify which lipids are bona fide direct agonists versus indirect modulators, refining our model of TRPV1 regulation in health and disease. The findings will directly inform the development of more selective TRPV1-targeted therapeutics.
proposals.novelty: While individual lipids have been studied, a systematic comparative analysis in both recombinant and native systems, combined with lipidomic profiling to identify requisite co-factors, has not been comprehensively performed. This approach directly tests the prevailing assumption of universal direct agonism.

proposals.title: Rational Design of Novel N-Acylethanolamine-Hydrolyzing Acid Amidase (NAAA) Inhibitors as Selective Endovanilloid Pathway Modulators for Inflammatory Pain
proposals.motivation: The endovanilloid system, mediated by TRPV1 receptors and endogenous ligands like N-oleoylethanolamine (OEA) and N-palmitoylethanolamine (PEA), is a key player in inflammatory and neuropathic pain. Pre-2026 pharmacology has focused on direct TRPV1 agonists/antagonists, which often cause side effects like hyperthermia. Targeting the biosynthesis and degradation of specific endovanilloids offers a more nuanced, potentially safer therapeutic strategy for pain management by modulating local ligand tone without globally blocking TRPV1.
proposals.hypothesis: Selective pharmacological inhibition of NAAA, the primary enzyme degrading PEA and OEA, will elevate local levels of these anti-inflammatory endovanilloids, leading to sustained TRPV1-mediated desensitization and analgesia in models of inflammatory pain, without the on-target side effects of systemic TRPV1 antagonism.
proposals.method: We will employ an in silico-guided drug design strategy, using homology modeling based on pre-2026 NAAA crystal structures and molecular docking to design novel β-lactone and urea-based inhibitors. Synthesized compounds will be screened for NAAA vs. Fatty Acid Amide Hydrolase (FAAH) selectivity in vitro using recombinant enzyme assays. Lead candidates will be tested in murine models of carrageenan-induced paw inflammation and CFA-induced arthritis, measuring pain behaviors (von Frey, Hargreaves test), tissue levels of PEA/OEA (LC-MS/MS), and markers of inflammation and TRPV1 activity in dorsal root ganglia neurons.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new chemical toolset of selective NAAA inhibitors, clarifying the therapeutic potential of endovanilloid pathway modulation. It will provide direct in vivo evidence linking selective elevation of PEA/OEA to analgesic and anti-inflammatory efficacy, advancing the field beyond direct receptor modulation towards a more refined control of endovanilloid signaling.
proposals.novelty: While NAAA inhibitors exist, this proposal uniquely focuses on their role as selective endovanilloid pathway modulators (distinct from endocannabinoid modulation) and employs a rational design approach to achieve unprecedented selectivity over FAAH, specifically to probe PEA/OEA-mediated analgesia without confounding CB1/CB2 effects.

proposals.title: Cell-autonomous dysregulation of the endocannabinoid system in human neurons: an iPSC-based twin model study
proposals.motivation: Variations in endocannabinoid (eCB) system components (e.g., CB1 receptor expression, 2-AG/AEA levels) are implicated in neuropsychiatric and metabolic disorders, but it is unclear to what extent these differences are genetically determined versus environmentally driven. Current human studies cannot disentangle these influences, and animal models lack human-specific neurobiology. A controlled, genetically-informed human neuronal model is needed to define the heritable core of eCB signaling variability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of the inter-individual variability in neuronal eCB system gene expression, receptor signaling, and ligand metabolism is cell-autonomous and can be captured in isogenic neurons derived from monozygotic twin pairs, revealing a genetically-determined eCB 'set-point'.
proposals.method: We will generate induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) from dermal fibroblasts of healthy monozygotic twin pairs (n=5 pairs). iPSCs will be differentiated into glutamatergic cortical neurons using established small-molecule protocols. In these isogenic neuronal cultures, we will quantify: 1) CB1 and TRPV1 receptor mRNA/protein levels (qPCR, Western blot), 2) functional receptor signaling (calcium imaging, cAMP assays), and 3) basal/activity-dependent levels of AEA and 2-AG (LC-MS/MS). Intra-pair correlations will be assessed against inter-pair variance to estimate cell-autonomous heritability.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel human neuronal platform to quantify the genetic contribution to eCB system variability, free from confounding systemic factors. It will provide foundational data on the 'intrinsic' eCB phenotype of human neurons, creating a benchmark for future studies on disease-associated mutations or pharmacogenetic responses in the eCB system.
proposals.novelty: While iPSC-neurons have been used to study eCBs in monogenic diseases, applying the twin model to this system to deconvolute genetic and environmental influences on eCB signaling in a controlled in vitro setting is a novel and powerful approach not yet reported in the literature.

proposals.title: Unbiased Pharmacogenetic Screening in Yeast Identifies Novel Genetic Modulators of Cannabidiol-Induced Lipid Perturbations
proposals.motivation: Cannabidiol (CBD) is widely used, yet its molecular mechanisms of action, particularly concerning its reported effects on lipid metabolism, remain incompletely understood and extend beyond known cannabinoid receptor pathways. An unbiased identification of the genetic determinants required for CBD's cellular effects is crucial for understanding its therapeutic potential and safety profile, especially given the clinical relevance of lipid modulation in metabolic and cardiovascular diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a genome-wide knockout screen in the model organism Saccharomyces cerevisiae will reveal conserved genes and pathways, distinct from canonical cannabinoid receptors, that are essential for mediating CBD's effects on cellular lipid levels and homeostasis.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-throughput pharmacogenetic screen using the yeast knockout collection. Yeast cultures will be treated with physiologically relevant concentrations of CBD, and lipid content will be assessed via a fluorescent dye-based assay (e.g., Nile Red) quantified by flow cytometry. Strains showing altered lipid accumulation profiles compared to wild-type will be identified. Hits will be validated in mammalian cell lines (e.g., C2C12 myoblasts or induced neurons) using siRNA-mediated knockdown of orthologous genes, followed by detailed lipidomic analysis via LC-MS to characterize specific lipid class alterations.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first unbiased genetic interaction map for CBD in a eukaryotic model, identifying novel molecular targets and pathways involved in its lipid-modulating effects. It will provide a mechanistic framework to explain CBD's pleiotropic actions and inform the development of more targeted therapeutics with reduced off-target effects.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has focused on known cannabinoid receptors, this proposal applies a systematic, genetics-first approach in yeast to discover entirely novel mediators of CBD action, moving beyond the candidate gene paradigm that has dominated the field.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Profiling of Endocannabinoid and Related Lipid Mediator Levels in Human Peripheral Blood Mononuclear Cell Subtypes: A Novel Biomarker Approach for Inflammatory States
proposals.motivation: The endocannabinoid system (ECS) is a crucial modulator of immune and neuronal function, but its role in human pathophysiology is poorly understood due to a lack of cellular resolution. Bulk tissue measurements of endocannabinoids (eCBs) like anandamide and 2-AG mask critical cell-type-specific dynamics, limiting our ability to define their precise roles in inflammation, neuro-immune communication, and metabolic disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct human immune cell subtypes (e.g., monocytes, T cells, B cells) possess unique eCB and related oxylipin 'lipid mediator' profiles, and that these profiles are significantly altered in patients with chronic low-grade inflammation (e.g., metabolic syndrome) compared to healthy controls.
proposals.method: We will isolate peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) from patients with metabolic syndrome and matched controls. Using fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS), we will isolate pure populations of CD14+ monocytes, CD3+ T cells, and CD19+ B cells. Intracellular levels of anandamide (AEA), 2-arachidonoylglycerol (2-AG), and related N-acylethanolamines (PEA, OEA) will be quantified using a highly sensitive liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) platform optimized for small sample volumes. Data will be analyzed using multivariate statistical methods (e.g., principal component analysis, PCA) to define cell-type-specific lipid signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative map of eCB levels in human primary immune cells at single-cell-type resolution. It will identify novel, cell-specific lipid biomarkers for inflammatory states and provide a foundational methodology for future studies on the ECS in human disease, moving beyond bulk tissue analysis.
proposals.novelty: While bulk plasma eCB measurements exist, no study has systematically quantified the full suite of eCBs and related mediators within sorted, pure human immune cell populations to define cell-intrinsic 'lipidomes' in health and disease.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Dual CB1 Receptor Antagonism/Agonism and TRPV1 Modulation of Tetrahydrocannabivarin (THCV) in Murine Models of Drug-Resistant Epilepsy
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research indicates that the phytocannabinoid THCV has a complex, dose-dependent pharmacological profile at CB1 receptors and may also modulate TRPV1 channels, both key targets in neurological disorders. However, its precise mechanism of action and therapeutic potential in central nervous system (CNS) diseases, particularly pharmacoresistant epilepsy, remains poorly defined despite promising anecdotal and preliminary data. Clarifying this mechanism is critical for rational drug design and for understanding the therapeutic window of cannabinoid-based therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that THCV exerts its anti-seizure effects through a combination of low-dose CB1 receptor antagonism, which may modulate excitatory/inhibitory balance, and concurrent activation of TRPV1 channels, contributing to neuroprotective and anti-convulsant outcomes in specific neural circuits.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-modal approach using established murine models of drug-resistant epilepsy (e.g., intrahippocampal kainate model). In vivo, we will assess seizure severity and EEG parameters following acute and chronic THCV administration. Ex vivo, we will use quantitative autoradiography and immunohistochemistry on brain sections to map CB1 and TRPV1 receptor expression and internalization. In primary neuronal cultures derived from these models, we will perform calcium imaging and patch-clamp electrophysiology to dissect THCV's direct effects on neuronal signaling via CB1 and TRPV1, using selective antagonists (e.g., AM251, capsazepine) to isolate contributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, mechanistic map of THCV's action in a relevant disease model, moving beyond descriptive studies. It will clarify its potential as a template for novel antiepileptic drugs with multi-target profiles. The findings will directly inform the GtoPdb database with precise ligand-receptor interaction data for THCV in a pathological context.
proposals.novelty: While THCV's biphasic CB1 activity is known in vitro, its integrated effect with TRPV1 modulation in a defined neurological disease model has not been systematically investigated. This proposal uniquely combines Di Marzo's expertise in endocannabinoid and TRPV1 signaling with detailed neuronal circuit analysis in epilepsy.

proposals.title: In Silico Discovery of Phytocannabinoid-Derived Negative Allosteric Modulators of the Cannabinoid CB1 Receptor
proposals.motivation: The therapeutic potential of CB1 receptor modulation is limited by the on-target adverse effects of orthosteric agonists and antagonists. Negative allosteric modulators (NAMs) offer a promising strategy to fine-tune CB1 signaling with greater spatial and temporal selectivity, potentially avoiding these side effects. However, the discovery of novel, potent, and selective CB1 NAMs, especially from natural product scaffolds, remains an underexplored area.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the diverse chemical space of phytocannabinoids and their derivatives contains scaffolds capable of selectively binding to known and putative allosteric sites on the CB1 receptor, thereby functioning as negative allosteric modulators.
proposals.method: We will employ a structure-based virtual screening pipeline. Using the active-state CB1 receptor structure (e.g., PDB ID 5TGZ) and known allosteric site data, we will define docking grids for the putative allosteric regions. A focused library of phytocannabinoids and synthetically accessible analogs will be prepared and docked using Glide (Schrödinger) and AutoDock Vina. Top-ranked hits will be subjected to molecular dynamics simulations (AMBER or GROMACS) to assess binding stability and allosteric communication via analysis of residue interaction networks and conformational changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify novel phytocannabinoid-based lead compounds for CB1 NAM development and provide structural insights into allosteric modulation mechanisms. It will expand the pharmacological toolkit for studying endocannabinoid system dysfunction and lay the groundwork for synthesizing and testing novel probes with potential therapeutic applications in metabolic and neurological disorders.
proposals.novelty: While computational screening for CB1 orthosteric ligands is common, this proposal specifically targets allosteric sites with a focused library of natural product scaffolds, bridging Di Marzo's expertise in endocannabinoid pharmacology with rational, structure-based drug design for allosteric modulators.

ideas.title: Computational Prediction and Design of Allosteric Polypharmacology for Cannabinoid Ligands
ideas.description: This research direction integrates computational polypharmacology modeling with the exploration of allosteric cannabinoid interactions to develop a novel predictive and design platform. The core objective is to use machine learning and structure-based modeling to forecast how cannabinoid ligands act as allosteric modulators at a network of non-CB GPCRs (e.g., serotonin, adenosine, or opioid receptors), predicting both the direction and strength of modulation. This synergy allows for the in silico design of new cannabinoid chemotypes with a desired 'allosteric polypharmacology fingerprint,' aiming to enhance therapeutic efficacy while minimizing adverse effects by precisely sculpting signaling across multiple receptor pathways. The ultimate goal is to generate novel, safer lead compounds with designed polypharmacology that leverages allosteric mechanisms for improved selectivity and reduced on-target toxicity.

ideas.title: Apply ecological network stability analysis to cannabinoid polypharmacology
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from systems ecology, where computational models analyze food web networks to predict the stability consequences of adding or removing a species. The analogous problem in pharmacology is forecasting the systemic, not just individual, off-target effects of a multi-target drug. The adapted research applies ecological network stability analysis to cannabinoid polypharmacology. The human proteome or key signaling pathways are modeled as an interaction network. Cannabinoid ligand profiles from polypharmacology models are introduced as targeted perturbations. The computational framework then simulates the dynamic effects, using ecological metrics like robustness, resilience, and cascade failure risk to predict the overall stability and functional consequences of the ligand's multi-target engagement, providing a systems-level forecast of adverse effect risk and therapeutic window.

ideas.title: Adapt High-Throughput Voltage-Sensitive Dye Arrays for Multiplexed Cannabinoid Receptor Signaling
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from cardiac electrophysiology and neuroscience, where high-throughput screening of cellular electrical activity is performed using multi-well microelectrode arrays or voltage-sensitive dye plates to record real-time kinetics from hundreds of samples. The analogous solution is adapted by replacing the measurement of electrical potentials with FRET-based biosensor readouts. The research idea involves engineering a multi-well plate platform where each well contains cells expressing a distinct FRET biosensor for a different cannabinoid receptor type (e.g., CB1, CB2, GPR55). A high-speed, multi-well FRET imaging plate reader would then be used to simultaneously measure real-time signaling dynamics across all these receptor types in parallel upon exposure to a library of cannabinoid ligands, enabling the rapid, comparative kinetic profiling of ligand-receptor interactions that was previously limited to sequential, single-receptor assays.

ideas.description: This idea adapts a spatial mapping solution from neuropharmacology, where mass spectrometry imaging (MSI) has been used to profile neurotransmitter enzyme distributions in brains with neurodegenerative disease. The analogous problem—understanding region-specific biochemical pathology in a complex organ—is solved by using MALDI-MSI to preserve spatial information while quantifying molecular targets. Adapted to the original seed idea, the research will measure expression of endocannabinoid synthetic/degradative enzymes not just in bulk islets but will spatially map their distribution across pancreatic tissue sections from type 2 diabetic donors. This approach will determine if enzyme changes are homogeneous or localized to specific micro-architectural regions (e.g., β-cell core vs. mantle, peri-islet acinar tissue), providing a higher-resolution understanding of endocannabinoid system dysregulation in diabetic pancreas pathology.

ideas.title: Inverse Multi-Target Design via Constrained Multi-Objective Optimization
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to a parallel inverse design problem from aerospace engineering: designing airfoils for specific, multi-faceted performance profiles. The analogous field's solution uses constrained multi-objective optimization to navigate a design space against conflicting criteria. Adapted to cannabinoid design, the idea is to computationally define the ligand chemical space, model activation at CB1, CB2, and key off-targets (e.g., 5-HT2A, adrenergic receptors) as separate objective functions, and apply multi-objective optimization algorithms. The goal is to inversely engineer candidate synthetic cannabinoids that achieve a user-defined, precise activation pattern (e.g., high CB2, moderate CB1, minimal off-target) by finding Pareto-optimal solutions that best satisfy all constraints and desired target profile weights.

ideas.title: A Multi-Scale Validation Framework for Cell Models Using an Organ-on-a-Chip Paradigm
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that cell lines can fully recapitulate primary cell behavior by introducing a solution from environmental toxicology: the use of organ-on-a-chip microphysiological systems. The analogous problem was the poor translation of static in vitro assays to in vivo outcomes, which was addressed by creating dynamic, mechanically active tissue culture environments. The adapted research idea is to develop dedicated microfluidic chips for primary mouse and human adipocytes and pancreatic islets that replicate key physiological cues like perfusion and mechanical strain. The core validation experiment then becomes a three-way comparison: assessing critical functional readouts in (1) the traditional cell lines (3T3-F442A, RIN-m5F) in standard culture, (2) primary cells in standard culture, and (3) primary cells in the organ-on-a-chip environment. This approach will precisely identify which aspects of primary cell behavior are inherently different from cell lines and, crucially, which of those differences are only unmasked by a more physiologically accurate microenvironment, thereby refining the validation paradigm itself.
